entries.doc: Proved for all sufficiently large sets (including the sharper version which characterises the case of equality) independently by Szegedy [Sz86] and Zaharescu [Za87]. The following is taken from [Sz86]. There exists an effectively computable $n_0$ with the following properties: (i) if $n \ge n_0$ and $a_1, a_2, \dots, a_n$ are distinct natural numbers then $\max_{i, j} \frac{a_i}{(a_i, a_j)} \ge n$
entries.statement: ∀ᶠ n in atTop, ∀ (A : Finset ℕ), A.card = n → 0 ∉ A → (n ≤ (A ×ˢ A).sup (fun x => x.1 / x.1.gcd x.2)) ∧ (n = (A ×ˢ A).sup (fun x => x.1 / x.1.gcd x.2) ↔ ∃ k > 0, A = (Finset.Icc 1 n).image (k * ·) ∨ A = (Finset.Icc 1 n).image (k * (Finset.Icc 1 n).lcm id / ·))

entries.name: erdos_477.variants.S_sq
entries.doc: There is no such $A$ for the polynomial $f(x) = X^2$. This is shown in [Sek59].
entries.statement: letI f := X ^ 2 ∀ A : Set ℤ, ∃ z, ¬ ∃! a ∈ A ×ˢ (f.eval '' {n | 0 < n}), z = a.1 + a.2

entries.name: erdos_477.variants.degree_two_dvd_condition_b_ne_zero
entries.doc: There is no such $A$ for any polynomial $f(x) = aX^2 + bX + c$, if $a | b$ with $a \ne 0$ and $b \ne 0. This was found be AlphaProof for the specific instance $X^2 - X + 1$ and then generalised.
entries.statement: ℤ} (ha : a ≠ 0) (hb : b ≠ 0) (hab : a ∣ b) : let f := a • X ^ 2 + b • X + C c ∀ A : Set ℤ, ∃ z, ¬ ∃! a ∈ A ×ˢ (f.eval '' {n | 0 < n}), z = a.1 + a.2

entries.name: erdos_503.variants.R2
entries.doc: When $n = 2$, the answer is 6 (due to Kelly [ErKe47] - an alternative proof is given by Kovács [Ko24c]). [ErKe47] Erdős, Paul and Kelly, L. M., Elementary Problems and Solutions: Solutions: E735. Amer. Math. Monthly (1947), 227-229. [Ko24c] Z. Kovács, A note on Erdős's mysterious remark. arXiv:2412.05190 (2024).
entries.statement: IsGreatest {(A.ncard) | (A : Set ℝ²) (hA : A.IsIsosceles)} 6

entries.name: erdos_503.variants.R3
entries.doc: When $n = 3$, the answer is 8 (due to Croft [Cr62]). [Cr62] Croft, H. T., $9$-point and $7$-point configurations in $3$-space. Proc. London Math. Soc. (3) (1962), 400-424.
entries.statement: IsGreatest {(A.ncard) | (A : Set ℝ³) (hA : A.IsIsosceles)} 8

entries.name: erdos_503.variants.upper_bound
entries.doc: The best upper bound known in general is due to Blokhius [Bl84] who showed that $$ |A| \leq \binom{n + 2}{2} $$ [Bl84] Blokhuis, A., Few-distance sets. (1984), iv+70.
entries.statement: ℕ) : ∀ m ∈ {(A.ncard) | (A : Set (ℝ^n)) (hA : A.IsIsosceles)}, m ≤ (n + 2).choose 2

entries.name: erdos_503.variants.lower_bound
entries.doc: Alweiss has observed a lower bound of $\binom{n + 1}{2}$ follows from considering the subset of $\mathbb{R}^{n + 1}$ formed of all vectors $e_i + e_j$ where $e_i$, $e_j$ are distinct coordinate vectors. This set can be viewed as a subset of some $\mathbb{R}^n$, and is easily checked to have the required property.
entries.statement: ℕ) : (n + 1).choose 2 ≤ sSup {(A.ncard) | (A : Set (ℝ^n)) (hA : A.IsIsosceles)}

entries.name: erdos_505.small_dim
entries.doc: **Erdős Problem 505** (disproved). Borsuk's conjecture is false for sufficiently large $n$: there exists a dimension $n$ and a bounded set $S \subseteq \mathbb{R}^n$ with positive diameter such that $S$ cannot be covered by $n + 1$ subsets each of diameter strictly less than $\operatorname{diam}(S)$. Erdős [Er44] suspected this. Disproved by Kahn–Kalai [KK93] for $n \geq 2015$. Currently known to
entries.statement: ℕ) (hn : n ≤ 3) (S : Set (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin n))) (hS : Bornology.IsBounded S) (hd : 0 < diam S) : ∃ (F : Fin (n + 1) → Set (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin n))), S ⊆ ⋃ i, F i ∧ ∀ i, diam (F i) < diam S

entries.name: erdos_507.variants.upper_trivial
entries.doc: It is trivial that $\alpha(n) \ll 1/n$.
entries.statement: α ≪ (fun n ↦ 1 / (n : ℝ))

entries.name: erdos_507.variants.lower_erdos
entries.doc: Erdős observed that $\alpha(n) \gg 1/n^2$.

entries.name: HadwigerNelsonAtLeastFive
entries.doc: Aubrey de Grey improved the lower bound for the chromatic number of the plane to 5 in 2018 using a graph that has >1000 nodes. "The chromatic number of the plane is at least 5" Aubrey D. N. J. de Grey, 2018 (https://doi.org/10.48550/arXiv.1804.02385)
entries.statement: 5 ≤ χ(ℝ²)

entries.name: erdos_517.variants.fejer
entries.doc: If `f(z) = ∑ aₖzⁿₖ` is an entire function (with `aₖ ≠ 0` for all `k`) such that `∑ 1 / nₖ < ∞`, then `f` assumes every value infinitely often. This theorem is proved in [Bi28].
entries.statement: ℂ → ℂ} {n : ℕ → ℕ} (hn : HasFejerGaps n) {a : ℕ → ℂ} (ha : ∀ k, a k ≠ 0) (hf : ∀ z, HasSum (fun k => a k * z ^ n k) (f z)) (z : ℂ) : {x : ℂ | f x = z}.Infinite

entries.name: erdos_590
entries.doc: Let $α$ be the infinite ordinal $\omega^{\omega}$. It was proved by Chang [Ch72] that any red/blue colouring of the edges of $K_α$ there is either a red $K_α$ or a blue $K_3$.
entries.statement: OrdinalCardinalRamsey (ω ^ ω) (ω ^ ω) 3

entries.name: erdos_590.variants.two
entries.doc: Specker [Sp57] proved that when $α=ω^2$ any red/blue colouring of the edges of $K_α$ there is either a red $K_α$ or a blue $K_3$.
entries.statement: OrdinalCardinalRamsey (ω ^ 2) (ω ^ 2) 3

entries.name: erdos_590.variants.ge_three_false
entries.doc: Specker [Sp57] proved that when $α=ω^n$ for $3≤ n < \omega$ then it is not the case that any red/blue colouring of the edges of $K_α$ there is either a red $K_α$ or a blue $K_3$.
entries.statement: ℕ} (h : 3 ≤ n) : ¬ OrdinalCardinalRamsey (ω ^ n) (ω ^ n) 3

entries.name: erdos_590.variants.finite_cardinal
entries.doc: Let m be a finite cardinal $< \omega$. Let $α$ be the infinite ordinal $\omega^{\omega}$. It was proved by Milnor that any red/blue colouring of the edges of $K_α$ there is either a red $K_α$ or a blue $K_3$. A shorter proof was found by Larson [La73]
entries.statement: ℕ) : OrdinalCardinalRamsey (ω ^ ω) (ω ^ ω) m

entries.name: erdos_591
entries.doc: Let $α$ be the infinite ordinal $\omega^{\omega^2}$. Is it true that any red/blue colouring of the edges of $K_α$ there is either a red $K_α$ or a blue $K_3$? This is true and was proved independently by Schipperus [Sc10] and Darby.
entries.statement: answer(True) ↔ OrdinalCardinalRamsey (ω ^ ω ^ 2) (ω ^ ω ^ 2) 3

entries.name: erdos_613
entries.doc: **Erdős Problem 613:** Let $n \geq 3$ and $G$ be a graph with $\binom{2n+1}{2} - \binom{n}{2} - 1$ edges. Must $G$ be the union of a bipartite graph and a graph with maximum degree less than $n$?
entries.statement: answer(False) ↔ ∀ n ≥ 3, ∀ (V : Type*) [Fintype V] (G : SimpleGraph V), [DecidableRel G.Adj] → G.edgeFinset.card = Nat.choose (2 * n + 1) 2 - Nat.choose n 2 - 1 → ∃ (B D : SimpleGraph V), [DecidableRel B.Adj] → [DecidableRel D.Adj] → G = B ⊔ D ∧ B.IsBipartite ∧ ∀ v, D.degree v < n

entries.name: erdos_617.variants.r_eq_3
entries.doc: Erdős and Gyárfás [ErGy99] proved the conjecture for $r=3$.
entries.statement: ℕ) (hr : r ≥ 3) {V : Type} [Fintype V] [DecidableEq V] (hV : Fintype.card V = 3^2 + 1) (coloring : Sym2 V → Fin 3) : ∃ (S : Finset V) (k : Fin 3), S.card = 3 + 1 ∧ ∀ u ∈ S, ∀ v ∈ S, u ≠ v → coloring s(u, v) ≠ k

entries.name: erdos_617.variants.r_eq_4
entries.doc: Erdős and Gyárfás [ErGy99] proved the conjecture for $r=4$.
entries.statement: ℕ) (hr : r ≥ 3) {V : Type} [Fintype V] [DecidableEq V] (hV : Fintype.card V = 4^2 + 1) (coloring : Sym2 V → Fin 4) : ∃ (S : Finset V) (k : Fin 4), S.card = 4 + 1 ∧ ∀ u ∈ S, ∀ v ∈ S, u ≠ v → coloring s(u, v) ≠ k

entries.name: erdos_617.variants.r2
entries.doc: Erdős and Gyárfás [ErGy99] showed this property fails for infinitely many $r$ if we replace $r^2+1$ by $r^2$.
entries.statement: {r : ℕ | ∃ (V : Type) (_ : Fintype V) (_ : DecidableEq V), Fintype.card V = r^2 ∧ ∃ (coloring : Sym2 V → Fin r), ∀ (S : Finset V), S.card = r + 1 → ∀ (k : Fin r), ∃ u ∈ S, ∃ v ∈ S, u ≠ v ∧ coloring s(u, v) = k}.Infinite

entries.name: erdos_659
entries.doc: Is there a set of $n$ points in $\mathbb{R}^2$ such that every subset of $4$ points determines at least $3$ distances, yet the total number of distinct distances is $\ll \frac{n}{\sqrt{\log n}}$? There does exist such a set: a suitable truncation of the lattice $\{(a,b\sqrt{2}): a,b\in\mathbb{Z}\}$ suffices. This construction appears to have been first considered by Moree and Osburn \cite{MoOs06},
entries.statement: answer(True) ↔ ∃ A : ℕ → Finset ℝ², (∀ n, #(A n) = n ∧ ∀ S ⊆ A n, #S = 4 → 3 ≤ distinctDistances S) ∧ (fun n ↦ distinctDistances (A n)) ≪ fun n ↦ n / sqrt (log n)

entries.name: erdos_694
entries.doc: Let $f_\max(n)$ be the largest $m$ such that $\phi(m) = n$, and $f_\min(n)$ be the smallest such $m$, where $\phi$ is Euler's totient function. Investigate $$ \max_{n\leq x}\frac{f_\max(n)}{f_\min(n)}. $$ GPT-5.5 Pro (prompted by Price) has proved (see also the comments for a summary) that $$ \max_{n\leq x}\frac{f_{\max}(n)}{f_{\min}(n)}=(e^\gamma+o(1))\log\log x. $$ A Lean formalisation of the re
entries.statement: ∀ᵉ (fmax : ℕ → ℕ) (fmin : ℕ → ℕ), (∀ n, IsGreatest (Nat.totient ⁻¹' {n}) (fmax n)) → (∀ n, IsLeast (Nat.totient ⁻¹' {n}) (fmin n)) → ∃ o : ℕ → ℝ, Tendsto o atTop (𝓝 0) ∧ ∀ x : ℕ, sSup { (fmax n : ℝ) / fmin n | (n : ℕ) (_ : n ≤ x) (_ : ∃ m, Nat.totient m = n) } = (exp eulerMascheroniConstant + o x) * log (log (x : ℝ))

entries.name: erdos_89.variants.n_dvd_log_n
entries.doc: Guth and Katz [GuKa15] proved that there are always $\gg \frac{n}{\log n}$ many distinct distances.
entries.statement: (fun (n : ℕ) => n/(n : ℝ).log) =O[atTop] (fun n => (minimalDistinctDistances n : ℝ))

entries.name: erdos_89.variants.grid_upper_bound
entries.doc: The square grid construction, going back to Erdős and Moser, shows that $\frac{n}{\sqrt{\log n}}$ is the correct order if the conjecture is true: there are configurations whose number of distinct distances is $O(\frac{n}{\sqrt{\log n}})$.
entries.statement: (fun n => (minimalDistinctDistances n : ℝ)) =O[atTop] (fun (n : ℕ) => n/(n : ℝ).log.sqrt)

entries.name: erdos_899
entries.doc: Let $A\subseteq\mathbb{N}$ be an infinite set such that $|A\cap \{1, ..., N\}| = o(N)$. Is it true that $$ \limsup_{N\to\infty}\frac{|(A - A)\cap \{1, ..., N\}|}{|A \cap \{1, ..., N\}|} = \infty? $$ The answer is yes, proved by Ruzsa [Ru78]. [Ru78] Ruzsa, I. Z., _On the cardinality of {$A+A$}\ and {$A-A$}_. (1978), 933--938.
entries.statement: answer(True) ↔ ∀ (A : Set ℕ), A.Infinite → Tendsto (fun N => (A ∩ Icc 1 N |>.ncard : ℝ) / N) atTop (𝓝 0) → atTop.limsup (fun N => ((A - A : Set ℕ) ∩ Icc 1 N |>.ncard : EReal) / (A ∩ Icc 1 N).ncard) = ⊤

entries.name: sawin_totally_real_tower
entries.doc: **Sawin's Lemmas 11–12 / Remarks Proposition 2.3: the totally real tower.** There exist $rdBound : \mathbb{R}$ and a *single* infinite set $Q$ of rational primes $q \equiv 1 \pmod 4$ such that for every $N$ one can find a totally real number field $F/\mathbb{Q}$ of degree $\ge N$ with bounded root discriminant $|disc F|^{1/[F:\mathbb{Q}]} \le rdBound$ in which *every* prime $q \in Q$ splits comple
entries.statement: ∃ (rdBound : ℝ) (Q : Set ℕ), Q.Infinite ∧ (∀ q ∈ Q, q.Prime ∧ q % 4 = 1) ∧ ∀ N : ℕ, ∃ (F : Type) (_ : Field F) (_ : CharZero F) (_ : NumberField F) (_ : IsTotallyReal F), N ≤ Module.finrank ℚ F ∧ (|(NumberField.discr F : ℝ)|) ^ ((1 : ℝ) / Module.finrank ℚ F) ≤ rdBound ∧ ∀ q ∈ Q, ∃ (factors : Finset (Ideal (𝓞 F))), factors.card = Module.finrank ℚ F ∧ ∀ p ∈ factors, p.IsMaximal ∧ (q : 𝓞 F) ∈ p

entries.name: erdos_931.variants.additional_condition_nonempty
entries.doc: In fact there exist counterexamples, like this one found by AlphaProof.
entries.statement: ∃ (k₁ k₂ : ℕ), ∃ (_h₁ : k₂ ≤ k₁), ∃ (_h₂ : 3 ≤ k₂), {(n₁, n₂) | n₁ + k₁ ≤ n₂ ∧ n₂ ≤ 2 * (n₁ + k₁) ∧ (∏ i ∈ Finset.Icc 1 k₁, (n₁ + i)).primeFactors = (∏ j ∈ Finset.Icc 1 k₂, (n₂ + j)).primeFactors}.Nonempty

entries.name: erdos_973.variants.m2_bounds
entries.doc: In [Er92f] (a different) Erdős refines this analysis, proving that if $M_2=\min_{z_j} \max_{2\leq k\leq n+1} \left\lvert \sum_{1\leq j\leq n}z_j^k\right\rvert$ where the minimum is taken over all $z_j\in \mathbb{C}$ with $\max \lvert z_j\rvert=1$, then $(1.746)^{-n} < M_2 < (1.745)^{-n}$.

entries.name: erdos_978.parts.i
entries.doc: Let `f ∈ ℤ[X]` be an irreducible polynomial with positive leading coefficient. Suppose that the degree `k` of `f` is larger than `2`, is not equal to a power of `2`, and `f n` has no fixed `(k - 1)`-th power divisors other than `1`. Then the set of `n` such that `f n` is `(k - 1)`-th power free has positive density, and this is proved in [Ho67].
entries.statement: ℤ[X]} (hi : Irreducible f) (hd : 2 < f.natDegree) (hp2 : ∀ (x : ℕ), f.natDegree ≠ 2 ^ x) (hlc : 0 < f.leadingCoeff) (hp : ∀ (p : ℕ), p.Prime → ∃ n : ℕ, ¬ (p : ℤ) ^ (f.natDegree - 1) ∣ f.eval (n : ℤ)) : HasPosDensity {n : ℕ | Powerfree (f.natDegree - 1) (f.eval (n : ℤ))}

entries.name: green_23
entries.doc: Suppose that $\mathbb{N}$ is finitely coloured. Are there $x,y$ of the same colour such that $x^2 + y^2$ is a square? Solved in [FrKlMo25].
entries.statement: answer(True) ↔ -- For every finite colouring of the natural numbers ∀ (k : ℕ) (c : ℕ → Fin k), -- there exist two numbers of the same colour whose squares sum to a square ∃ x y : ℕ, 0 < x ∧ 0 < y ∧ -- Exclude trivial x^2 + 0^2 = x^2 solution c x = c y ∧ -- Same colour IsSquare (x^2 + y^2)

entries.name: green_44.variants.less_than_sqrt
entries.doc: The answer is affirmative if the primes are all less than $N^{1/2}$, by the large sieve. [Gr24]
entries.statement: ∀ (N : ℕ) (p : Fin 1000 → ℕ) (A : (i : Fin 1000) → Finset (ZMod (p i))), let remaining := (Finset.Icc 1 N).filter (fun x => ∀ i, (x : ZMod (p i)) ∉ A i) (∀ i, (p i).Prime) → StrictMono p → (p 999) ^ 2 < N → (∀ i, (A i).card = (p i) / 2) → 10 * remaining.card ≤ N

entries.name: green_49
entries.doc: The vector space $\mathbb{F}_2^n$. -/ abbrev 𝔽₂ (n : ℕ) := Fin n → ZMod 2 /-- Suppose that $A \subset \mathbb{F}_2^n$ is a set with $|A + A| \leq K|A|$. Is it true that $A$ is covered by $K^{O(1)}$ translates of a subspace of size $\leq |A|$? Solved by [GGM25].
entries.statement: answer(True) ↔ ∃ C > 0, ∀ n (A : Finset (𝔽₂ n)), A.Nonempty → ∀ K ≥ (1 : ℝ), (#(A + A) : ℝ) ≤ K * #A → ∃ (W : Submodule (ZMod 2) (𝔽₂ n)) (T : Finset (𝔽₂ n)), Nat.card W ≤ #A ∧ (#T : ℝ) ≤ K ^ C ∧ (A : Set (𝔽₂ n)) ⊆ T + W

entries.statement: SimpleGraph α) [DecidableRel G.Adj] (h_conn: G.Connected) (h : G.CliqueFree 3) : ∃ (β : Type*) (_ : Fintype β) (H : SimpleGraph β) (_ : DecidableRel H.Adj) (i : G ↪g H), H.CliqueFree 3 ∧ degreeSequenceMultiplicity H = 3

entries.name: GromovPolynomialGrowthTheorem
entries.doc: Infinite groups do not satisfy polynomial growth over `ℕ` for any degree `d` because when `d = 0` this reduces to the unbounded nature of `growthFunction` while `n = 0` works when `d ≠ 0`. Thus a finitely-generated infinite nilpotent group would be a counter-example to Gromov's theorem when quantifying over all of `ℕ`, and so `n = 0` should be excluded. -/ @[category test, AMS 20] theorem growthFu
entries.statement: HasPolynomialGrowth G ↔ Group.IsVirtuallyNilpotent G

entries.name: sendov_conjecture.variants.le_nine
entries.doc: **Sendov's conjecture** states that for a polynomial $$f(z)=(z-r_{1})\cdots (z-r_{n}),\qquad (n\geq 2)$$ with all roots $r_1, ..., r_n$ inside the closed unit disk $|z| ≤ 1$, each of the $n$ roots is at a distance no more than $1$ from at least one critical point. It has been shown that Sendov's conjecture holds when the degree of $n$ is at most $9$.
entries.statement: ℕ) (hn : n ∈ Set.Icc 2 9) : n.SatisfiesSendovConjecture

entries.name: sendov_conjecture.variants.eventually_true
entries.doc: **Sendov's conjecture** states that for a polynomial $$f(z)=(z-r_{1})\cdots (z-r_{n}),\qquad (n\geq 2)$$ with all roots $r_1, ..., r_n$ inside the closed unit disk $|z| ≤ 1$, each of the $n$ roots is at a distance no more than $1$ from at least one critical point. It has been shown that Sendov's conjecture holds for polynomials of sufficiently large degree.
entries.statement: ∀ᶠ (n : ℕ) in Filter.atTop, n.SatisfiesSendovConjecture

entries.name: sidorenko_K22
entries.doc: `edgeCount` of `K_{2,2}` (complete bipartite graph on `Fin 2 + Fin 2`) is `4`. The four edges are `{inl 0, inr 0}`, `{inl 0, inr 1}`, `{inl 1, inr 0}`, `{inl 1, inr 1}`. -/ @[category API, AMS 5] lemma edgeCount_completeBipartiteGraph_fin_two : (completeBipartiteGraph (Fin 2) (Fin 2)).edgeFinset.card = 4 := by -- Every vertex of `K_{2,2}` has degree 2, and there are 4 vertices; so by the -- handsh
entries.statement: Type} [Fintype W] [DecidableEq W] (G : SimpleGraph W) [DecidableRel G.Adj] : homDensity (completeGraph (Fin 2)) G ^ ((completeBipartiteGraph (Fin 2) (Fin 2)).edgeFinset.card) ≤ homDensity (completeBipartiteGraph (Fin 2) (Fin 2)) G

entries.name: sidorenko_tree_subsingleton
entries.doc: Consequence of `homDensity_le_one`: both sides of the Sidorenko K_{2,2} inequality are bounded above by 1. This is a trivial consequence, recorded as a sanity check on the helper infrastructure in `FormalConjecturesForMathlib.Combinatorics.SimpleGraph.HomDensity`. -/ @[category API, AMS 5] theorem sidorenko_K22_both_sides_bounded {W : Type*} [Fintype W] [Nonempty W] [DecidableEq W] (G : SimpleGrap
entries.statement: Type} [Fintype V] [Fintype W] [Subsingleton V] [DecidableEq V] [DecidableEq W] [Nonempty W] (H : SimpleGraph V) (G : SimpleGraph W) [DecidableRel H.Adj] [DecidableRel G.Adj] : homDensity (completeGraph (Fin 2)) G ^ (H.edgeFinset.card) ≤ homDensity H G

entries.name: sidorenko_tree
entries.doc: **Case: `H` is a tree (Sidorenko 1993).** If `H` is a finite tree then Sidorenko's inequality holds. **Proof idea (Sidorenko 1993):** by induction on the tree, applying Jensen / convexity. The number of homomorphisms from a tree `T` with `v` vertices and `v - 1` edges into `G` factors nicely in the degree sequence of `G`, and AM–GM / convexity gives the required lower bound. **Current status (2026
entries.statement: Type} [Fintype V] [Fintype W] [DecidableEq V] [DecidableEq W] [Nonempty W] (H : SimpleGraph V) (G : SimpleGraph W) [DecidableRel H.Adj] [DecidableRel G.Adj] (hTree : H.IsTree) : homDensity (completeGraph (Fin 2)) G ^ (H.edgeFinset.card) ≤ homDensity H G

entries.name: conjecture32
entries.doc: WOWII [Conjecture 32](http://cms.dt.uh.edu/faculty/delavinae/research/wowII/) For a simple connected graph $G$, $\operatorname{path}(G) \ge \operatorname{dist}\_{\operatorname{avg}}(A) + 0.5 \cdot \operatorname{ecc}\_{\operatorname{avg}}(M)$, where $\operatorname{path}(G)$ is the floor of the average distance of $G$, $A$ is the set of minimum-degree vertices, $M$ is the set of maximum-degree verti
entries.statement: answer(False) ↔ ∀ (G : SimpleGraph α) [DecidableRel G.Adj] (h : G.Connected), let A : Finset α := Finset.univ.filter (fun v => G.degree v = G.minDegree) let M : Finset α := Finset.univ.filter (fun v => G.degree v = G.maxDegree) let eccavg (S : Finset α) : ℝ := (∑ v ∈ S, (G.eccent v).toNat) / (S.card : ℝ) distavg G A + (1 / 2 : ℝ) * eccavg M ≤ (path G : ℝ)

entries.name: conjecture5
entries.doc: WOWII [Conjecture 5](http://cms.dt.uh.edu/faculty/delavinae/research/wowII/) For a simple connected graph `G`, `Ls(G)` is bounded below by the maximal size of a sphere of radius `radius(G)` around the centres of `G`.
entries.statement: SimpleGraph V) (h_conn : G.Connected) : letI centers := { v : V | G.eccent v = G.radius } letI r_nat := G.radius.toNat letI sphere_verts (v : V) : Set V := { w | G.dist v w = r_nat } letI sphere_size (v : V) : ℝ := ↑(Finset.univ.filter (fun w => w ∈ sphere_verts v)).card letI max_sphere_size := sSup (sphere_size '' centers) max_sphere_size ≤ Ls G

entries.name: conjecture7
entries.doc: WOWII [Conjecture 7](http://cms.dt.uh.edu/faculty/delavinae/research/wowII/) For a simple connected graph $G$, $L_s(G) \ge \max_v \lambda(v) - 1 + n - 2 \alpha(G)$, where $L_s(G)$ is the maximum number of leaves over all spanning trees of $G$, $n = |V(G)|$, $\alpha(G) = G.\mathrm{indepNum}$ is the independence number, and $\lambda(v) = \mathrm{indepNeighborsCard}\, G\, v$ is the independence numbe
entries.statement: SimpleGraph α) [DecidableRel G.Adj] (h : G.Connected) : let maxL := (Finset.univ.image (fun v => indepNeighborsCard G v)).max' (by simp) ((maxL : ℤ) - 1 + (Fintype.card α : ℤ) - 2 * (G.indepNum : ℤ) : ℝ) ≤ Ls G

entries.name: erdos_1175.variants.shelah_consistency
entries.doc: **Shelah's consistency result**: it is consistent with ZFC that there exists a graph $G$ with chromatic number $\aleph_1$ such that every triangle-free subgraph of $G$ has chromatic number strictly less than $\aleph_1$. This shows that a negative answer to Problem 1175 (with $\kappa = \lambda = \aleph_1$) is consistent, so the main statement `erdos_1175` is not provable in ZFC. **Formalization cav
entries.statement: answer(sorry) ↔ ¬ (∀ (V : Type*) (G : SimpleGraph V), G.chromaticCardinal = ℵ_ 1 → ∃ (H : G.Subgraph), H.coe.CliqueFree 3 ∧ H.coe.chromaticCardinal = ℵ_ 1)

entries.name: oddOrderLeq9LatinSquareTransversal
entries.doc: The conjecture is known to be true for $n \leq 9$.
entries.statement: answer(sorry) ↔ ∀ n ≤ 9, Odd n → ∀ (L : LatinSquare n), ∃ σ, IsTransversal L σ

entries.name: isAccumulationPoint_three_halves_pow_infinite
entries.doc: The sequence `(3/2)^n` has infinitely many accumulation points modulo `1`.
entries.statement: {x | IsAccumulationPoint x (fun n => Int.fract <| (3 / 2 : ℝ)^n)}.Infinite

entries.name: erdos_1.variants.lb
entries.doc: The trivial lower bound is $N \gg 2^n / n$. -/ @[category textbook, AMS 5 11] theorem erdos_1.variants.weaker : ∃ C > (0 : ℝ), ∀ (N : ℕ) (A : Finset ℕ) (_ : IsSumDistinctSet A N), N ≠ 0 → C * 2 ^ A.card / A.card < N := by refine ⟨1/3, by norm_num, fun N A ⟨hA1, hA2⟩ hN => ?_⟩ have key : 2 ^ A.card ≤ A.card * N + 1 := by rw [← Finset.card_powerset] exact (Finset.card_le_card_of_injOn (Finset.sum ·
entries.statement: ∃ (o : ℕ → ℝ) (_ : o =o[atTop] (1 : ℕ → ℝ)), ∀ (N : ℕ) (A : Finset ℕ) (h : IsSumDistinctSet A N), (1 / 4 - o A.card) * 2 ^ A.card / (A.card : ℝ).sqrt ≤ N

entries.name: erdos_1.variants.lb_strong
entries.doc: A number of improvements of the constant $\frac{1}{4}$ have been given, with the current record $\sqrt{2 / \pi}$ first provied in unpublished work of Elkies and Gleason.
entries.statement: ∃ (o : ℕ → ℝ) (_ : o =o[atTop] (1 : ℕ → ℝ)), ∀ (N : ℕ) (A : Finset ℕ) (h : IsSumDistinctSet A N), (√(2 / π) - o A.card) * 2 ^ A.card / (A.card : ℝ).sqrt ≤ N

entries.name: erdos_1.variants.least_N_5
entries.doc: The minimal value of $N$ such that there exists a sum-distinct set with three elements is $4$. https://oeis.org/A276661 -/ @[category textbook, AMS 5 11] theorem erdos_1.variants.least_N_3 : IsLeast { N | ∃ A, IsSumDistinctSet A N ∧ A.card = 3 } 4 := by refine ⟨⟨{1, 2, 4}, ?_⟩, ?_⟩ · simp refine ⟨by decide, ?_⟩ let P := Finset.powerset {1, 2, 4} have : Finset.univ.image (fun p : P ↦ ∑ x ∈ p, x) =
entries.statement: IsLeast { N | ∃ A, IsSumDistinctSet A N ∧ A.card = 5 } 13

entries.name: erdos_1.variants.least_N_9
entries.doc: The minimal value of $N$ such that there exists a sum-distinct set with nine elements is $161$. https://oeis.org/A276661
entries.statement: IsLeast { N | ∃ A, IsSumDistinctSet A N ∧ A.card = 9 } 161

entries.name: erdos_10.variants.gallagher
entries.doc: Gallagher [Ga75] has shown that for any $ϵ > 0$ there exists $k(ϵ)$ such that the set of integers which are the sum of a prime and at most $k(ϵ)$ many powers of $2$ has lower density at least $1 - ϵ$. Ref: Gallagher, P. X., _Primes and powers of 2_.
entries.statement: ℝ) (hε : 0 < ε) : ∃ k, 1 - ε ≤ (sumPrimeAndTwoPows k).lowerDensity

entries.name: erdos_10.variants.grechuk_example
entries.doc: Bogdan Grechuk has observed that `1117175146` is not the sum of a prime and at most $3$ powers of $2$.
entries.statement: 1117175146 ∉ sumPrimeAndTwoPows 3

entries.name: erdos_10.variants.two_pows
entries.doc: There are infinitely many even integers not the sum of a prime and $2$ powers of $2$
entries.statement: Set.Infinite <| {n : ℕ | Even n} \ sumPrimeAndTwoPows 2

entries.name: erdos_1004.variants.le_of_isDistinctTotientRun
entries.doc: Erdős, Pomerance, and Sárközy [EPS87] proved that if φ(n+k) are all distinct for 1 ≤ k ≤ K then K ≤ n / exp(c (log n)^{1/3}) for some constant c > 0. Here we state the existence of such a constant c.
entries.statement: answer(True) ↔ ∃ (c : ℝ) (hc : c > 0), ∀ᶠ n in atTop, ∀ (K : ℕ), IsDistinctTotientRun n K → (K : ℝ) ≤ (n : ℝ) / Real.exp (c * (Real.log n) ^ (1/3 : ℝ))

entries.name: erdos_1051.variants.rapid_growth
entries.doc: A sequence of integers `a` satisfies the growth condition if $\liminf a_n^{\frac{1}{2^n}} > 1$. -/ def GrowthCondition (a : ℕ → ℤ) : Prop := Filter.liminf (fun n => ((a n : ℝ) ^ (1 / 2 ^ n : ℝ))) Filter.atTop > 1 /-- The series $\sum_{n=0}^\infty \frac{1}{a_n \cdot a_{n+1}}$. -/ noncomputable def ErdosSeries (a : ℕ → ℤ) : ℝ := ∑' n : ℕ, 1 / ((a n : ℝ) * (a (n + 1) : ℝ)) /-- Is it true that if $a_0
entries.statement: ℕ → ℤ) (h_mono : StrictMono a) (h_rapid : ∃ C > 0, ∀ n, (a (n + 1) : ℝ) ≥ C * (a n : ℝ) ^ 2) : Irrational (ErdosSeries a)

entries.name: erdos_1062.variants.lower_bound
entries.doc: The interval `[⌊n/3⌋, n]` is fork-free, and therefore `f n` is at least `⌈2n / 3⌉`.

entries.name: erdos_1062.variants.lebensold_bounds
entries.doc: Lebensold proved that for large `n`, the function `f n` lies between `0.6725 n` and `0.6736 n`.
entries.statement: ∀ᶠ n in atTop, (0.6725 : ℝ) * n ≤ f n ∧ f n ≤ (0.6736 : ℝ) * n

entries.name: erdos_1063.variants.exists_exception
entries.doc: Erdős and Selfridge noted that, for $n \ge 2k$ with $k \ge 2$, at least one of the numbers $n - i$ for $0 \le i < k$ fails to divide $\binom{n}{k}$ ([ErSe83]).
entries.statement: ℕ} (hk : 2 ≤ k) (h : 2 * k ≤ n) : ∃ i < k, ¬ (n - i) ∣ n.choose k

entries.name: erdos_1063.variants.small_values
entries.doc: The initial values satisfy $n_2 = 4$, $n_3 = 6$, $n_4 = 9$, and $n_5 = 12$ ([Gu04], Problem B31).

entries.name: erdos_1063.variants.monier_upper_bound
entries.doc: Monier observed that $n_k \le k!$ for $k \ge 3$ ([Mo85]). TODO: Find reference
entries.statement: ℕ} (hk : 3 ≤ k) : n k ≤ k !

entries.name: erdos_1063.variants.cambie_upper_bound
entries.doc: [Cambie observed](https://www.erdosproblems.com/1063) the improved bound $n_k \le k \cdot \operatorname{lcm}(1, \dotsc, k - 1)$.
entries.statement: ℕ} (hk : 3 ≤ k) : n k ≤ k * (Finset.Icc 1 (k - 1)).lcm id

entries.name: erdos_1063.variants.exp_upper_bound
entries.doc: The least common multiple bound implies $n_k \le \exp((1 + o(1))k)$.
entries.statement: ∃ f : ℕ → ℝ, Tendsto f atTop (𝓝 0) ∧ ∀ k, (n k : ℝ) ≤ exp ((1 + f k) * k)

entries.name: erdos_1067.variants.infinite_edge_connectivity
entries.doc: A graph is infinitely edge-connected if to disconnect the graph requires deleting infinitely many edges. In other words, removing any finite set of edges leaves the graph connected. -/ def InfinitelyEdgeConnected {V : Type*} (G : SimpleGraph V) : Prop := ∀ ⦃s : Set (Sym2 V)⦄, s.Finite → (G.deleteEdges s).Connected /-- Does every graph with chromatic number $\aleph_1$ contain an infinitely connecte
entries.statement: answer(False) ↔ ∀ (V : Type) (G : SimpleGraph V), G.chromaticCardinal = ℵ_ 1 → ∃ (H : G.Subgraph), H.coe.chromaticCardinal = ℵ_ 1 ∧ InfinitelyEdgeConnected H.coe

entries.name: erdos_1074.variants.PillaiPrimes_infinite
entries.doc: Pillai [Pi30] raised the question of whether there exist any primes in $P$. This was answered by Chowla, who noted that, for example, $14! + 1 \equiv 18! + 1 \equiv 0 \pmod{23}$. -/ @[category test, AMS 11] theorem erdos_1074.variants.mem_pillaiPrimes : 23 ∈ PillaiPrimes := by norm_num exact ⟨14, by decide⟩ /-- Erdős, Hardy, and Subbarao proved that $S$ is infinite. Formal proof linked here provid
entries.statement: PillaiPrimes.Infinite

entries.name: f_asymptotic_2
entries.doc: $f_r(n)$ is maximal such that, if a graph $G$ on $n$ vertices has the property that every subgraph $H$ on $m$ vertices has chromatic number $\leq r$ once we remove $f_r(m)$ edges from it, then $G$ has chromatic number $\leq r+1$. -/ noncomputable def f (r n : ℕ) : ℕ := sSup {k : ℕ | ∀ G : SimpleGraph (Fin n), (∀ H : Subgraph G, ∃ E : Finset (Sym2 H.verts), E.card ≤ k ∧ chromaticNumber (H.coe.delet
entries.statement: answer(False) ↔ (fun (n : ℕ) => (n : ℝ)) =o[atTop] (fun (n : ℕ) => (f 2 n : ℝ))

entries.name: f_asymptotic_general
entries.doc: Is $f_r(n) \gg_r n$ for all $r$? Disproved by Rödl, who showed $f_r(n) = o(n)$ for all fixed $r \geq 2$.
entries.statement: answer(False) ↔ ∀ r : ℕ, (fun n : ℕ => ((r : ℝ) * n)) =o[atTop] (fun n : ℕ => (f r n : ℝ))

entries.name: erdos_1095.variants.lower_solved
entries.doc: Let $g(k)>k+1$ be the smallest $n$ such that all prime factors of $\binom{n}{k}$ are $>k$. -/ noncomputable def g (k : ℕ) : ℕ := sInf {m | k + 1 < m ∧ k < (m.choose k).minFac} -- TODO: Add erdos_1095. /-- The current record is $g(k) \gg \exp(c(\log k)^2)$ for some $c>0$, due to Konyagin [Ko99b]. -
entries.statement: ∃ c > 0, (fun k : ℕ ↦ exp (c * log k ^ 2)) =O[atTop] fun k ↦ (g k : ℝ)

entries.name: erdos_1104.variants.lower
entries.doc: Maximum chromatic number of a triangle-free graph on `n` vertices. -/ noncomputable def triangleFreeMaxChromatic (n : ℕ) : ℕ := sSup {χ | ∃ G : SimpleGraph (Fin n), G.CliqueFree 3 ∧ G.chromaticNumber = χ} -- TODO: Add erdos_1104. /-- Lower bound (Hefty–Horn–King–Pfender 2025). There exists a constant $c_1 \in (0,1]$ such that, for sufficiently large $n$, $$ c_1 \sqrt{\frac{n}{\log n}} \le f(n), $$
entries.statement: ∃ c₁ : ℝ, 0 < c₁ ∧ c₁ ≤ 1 ∧ (∀ᶠ n : ℕ in atTop, c₁ * Real.sqrt (n : ℝ) / Real.sqrt (Real.log (n : ℝ)) ≤ (triangleFreeMaxChromatic n : ℝ))

entries.name: erdos_1104.variants.upper
entries.doc: Upper bound (Davies–Illingworth 2022). There exists a constant $c_2 \ge 2$ such that, for sufficiently large $n$, $$ f(n) \le c_2 \sqrt{\frac{n}{\log n}}, $$ where $f(n)$ denotes the maximum chromatic number of a triangle-free graph on $n$ vertices, formalized as `triangleFreeMaxChromatic n`.
entries.statement: ∃ c₂ : ℝ, 2 ≤ c₂ ∧ (∀ᶠ n : ℕ in atTop, (triangleFreeMaxChromatic n : ℝ) ≤ c₂ * Real.sqrt (n : ℝ) / Real.sqrt (Real.log (n : ℝ)))

entries.name: erdos_1107.variants.two
entries.doc: Heath-Brown [He88] proved every large integer the sum of at most three $2$-powerful numbers.
entries.statement: ∀ᶠ n in atTop, SumOfRPowerful 2 n

entries.name: erdos_1128.prikryMills
entries.doc: A subset $A_1 \times B_1 \times C_1$ of $A \times B \times C$ is **monochromatic** under a 2-colouring $f : A \to B \to C \to \operatorname{Fin} 2$ if $f$ is constant on $A_1 \times B_1 \times C_1$. -/ def IsMonochromaticBox {A B C : Type*} (f : A → B → C → Fin 2) (A₁ : Set A) (B₁ : Set B) (C₁ : Set C) : Prop := ∃ c : Fin 2, ∀ a ∈ A₁, ∀ b ∈ B₁, ∀ c' ∈ C₁, f a b c' = c /- Auxiliary lemmas for the P
entries.statement: ∃ (X : Type) (_ : #X = aleph 1) (f : X → X → X → Fin 2), ∀ (A₁ B₁ C₁ : Set X), #A₁ = aleph 0 → #B₁ = aleph 0 → #C₁ = aleph 0 → ¬ IsMonochromaticBox f A₁ B₁ C₁

entries.name: erdos_1133.variants.weaker
entries.doc: Erdős proved that, for any $C>0$, there exists $\epsilon>0$ such that if $n$ is sufficiently large and $m=\lfloor (1+\epsilon)n\rfloor$ then for any $x_1,\ldots,x_m\in [-1,1]$ there is a polynomial $P$ of degree $n$ such that $\lvert P(x_i)\rvert\leq 1$ for $1\leq i\leq m$ and $\max_{x\in [-1,1]}\lvert P(x)\rvert>C$. The conjectured statement would also imply this, but Erdős in [Er67] says he coul
entries.statement: ∀ C > (0 : ℝ), ∃ ε > (0 : ℝ), ∀ᶠ n : ℕ in atTop, let m := ⌊(1 + ε) * (n : ℝ)⌋₊; ∀ x : Fin m → Icc (-1 : ℝ) 1, ∃ P : Polynomial ℝ, P.natDegree = n ∧ (∀ i : Fin m, |P.eval (x i : ℝ)| ≤ 1) ∧ ∃ z ∈ Icc (-1 : ℝ) 1, |P.eval z| > C

entries.name: erdos_1142.variants.mientka_weitzenkamp
entries.doc: Mientka and Weitzenkamp [MiWe69] proved that the only $n \leq 2^{44}$ such that $n > 2$ and $n - 2^k$ is prime for all $k \geq 1$ with $2^k < n$ are $4, 7, 15, 21, 45, 75, 105$.

entries.name: erdos_1148
entries.doc: A natural number $n$ which can be written as $n$ if $n = x^2 + y^2 - z^2$ with $\max(x^2, y^2, z^2) \leq n$. -/ def Erdos1148Prop (n : ℕ) : Prop := ∃ x y z : ℕ, n = x ^ 2 + y ^ 2 - z ^ 2 ∧ x ^ 2 ≤ n ∧ y ^ 2 ≤ n ∧ z ^ 2 ≤ n /-- Can every large integer $n$ be written as $n=x^2+y^2-z^2$ with $\max(x^2,y^2,z^2)\leq n$? This was proved affirmatively by Chojecki [Ch26], using a Duke-type equidistributio
entries.statement: answer(True) ↔ ∀ᶠ n in atTop, Erdos1148Prop n

entries.name: erdos_1148.variants.weaker
entries.doc: The largest integer known which cannot be written this way is $6563$. -/ private instance (n : ℕ) : Decidable (Erdos1148Prop n) := decidable_of_iff (∃ x ∈ Finset.range (Nat.sqrt n + 1), ∃ y ∈ Finset.range (Nat.sqrt n + 1), ∃ z ∈ Finset.range (Nat.sqrt n + 1), n = x ^ 2 + y ^ 2 - z ^ 2 ∧ x ^ 2 ≤ n ∧ y ^ 2 ≤ n ∧ z ^ 2 ≤ n) (by constructor · rintro ⟨x, -, y, -, z, -, h⟩; exact ⟨x, y, z, h⟩ · rintro ⟨
entries.statement: ∀ n, erdos_1148_weaker_prop n

entries.name: erdos_12.variants.erdos_sarkozy_density_0
entries.doc: Erdős and Sárközy proved that such an $A$ must have density 0. [ErSa70] Erd\H os, P. and Sárk\"ozi, A., On the divisibility properties of sequences of integers. Proc. London Math. Soc. (3) (1970), 97-101
entries.statement: Set ℕ) (hA : IsGood A) : A.HasDensity 0

entries.name: erdos_12.variants.erdos_sarkozy
entries.doc: Given any function $f(x)\to \infty$ as $x\to \infty$ there exists a set $A$ with the property that there are no distinct $a,b,c \in A$ such that $a \mid (b+c)$ and $b,c > a$, such that there are infinitely many $N$ such that $$\lvert A\cap\{1,\ldots,N\}\rvert > \frac{N}{f(N)}.
entries.statement: ℕ → ℕ) (hf : atTop.Tendsto f atTop) : ∃ A, IsGood A ∧ {N : ℕ | (N : ℝ) / f N < (A ∩ Icc 1 N).ncard}.Infinite

entries.name: erdos_12.variants.example
entries.doc: An example of an $A$ with the property that there are no distinct $a,b,c \in A$ such that $a \mid (b+c)$ and $b,c > a$ and such that $$\liminf \frac{\lvert A\cap\{1,\ldots,N\}\rvert}{N^{1/2}}\log N > 0$$ is given by the set of $p^2$, where $p\equiv 3\pmod{4}$ is prime.
entries.statement: Set ℕ) (hA : A = {p ^ 2 | (p : ℕ) (_ : p.Prime) (_ : p ≡ 3 [MOD 4])}) : IsGood A ∧ 0 < atTop.liminf (fun (N : ℕ) ↦ (A ∩ Icc 1 N).ncard * (N : ℝ).log / √N)

entries.name: erdos_12.variants.schoen
entries.doc: Let $A$ be a set of natural numbers with the property that there are no distinct $a,b,c \in A$ such that $a \mid (b+c)$ and $b,c > a$. If all elements in $A$ are pairwise coprime then $$\lvert A\cap\{1,\ldots,N\}\rvert \ll N^{2/3}$$
entries.statement: Set ℕ) (hA : IsGood A) (hA' : A.Pairwise Nat.Coprime) : (fun N ↦ ((A ∩ Icc 1 N).ncard : ℝ)) =O[atTop] (fun N ↦ (N : ℝ) ^ (2 / 3 : ℝ))

entries.name: erdos_12.variants.baier
entries.doc: Let $A$ be a set of natural numbers with the property that there are no distinct $a,b,c \in A$ such that $a \mid (b+c)$ and $b,c > a$. If all elements in $A$ are pairwise coprime then $$\lvert A\cap\{1,\ldots,N\}\rvert \ll N^{2/3}/\log N$$
entries.statement: Set ℕ) (hA : IsGood A) (hA' : A.Pairwise Nat.Coprime) : (fun N ↦ ((A ∩ Icc 1 N).ncard : ℝ)) =O[atTop] (fun N ↦ (N : ℝ) ^ (2 / 3 : ℝ) / (N : ℝ).log)

entries.name: erdos_120.variants.finite_set
entries.doc: Steinhaus [St20] has proved Erdős 120 to be false whenever $A$ is a finite set.
entries.statement: Set ℝ} (h : A.Finite) : ¬ Erdos120For A

entries.name: erdos_1201.variants.epsilon_half
entries.doc: Erdős wrote he could prove this for $\epsilon=1/2$.
entries.statement: ∀ η > 0, ∃ k : ℕ, atTop.liminf (fun x : ℕ ↦ (((count (Erdos1201Set (1 / 2 : ℝ) k) x : ℝ) / (x : ℝ)) : EReal)) ≥ (1 - η : EReal)

entries.name: erdos_1203.variants.lower_bound
entries.doc: It is easy to prove that $F(n)\geq 1-o(1)$.
entries.statement: ∀ ε > 0, ∀ᶠ n in atTop, F n ≥ 1 - ε

entries.name: erdos_123.variants.erdos_lewin_3_5_7
entries.doc: Erdős and Lewin proved this conjecture when $a = 3$, $b = 5$, and $c = 7$. Reference: [ErLe96] Erdős, P. and Lewin, Mordechai, _$d$-complete sequences of integers_. Math. Comp. (1996), 837-840.
entries.statement: IsDComplete (↑(powers 3) * ↑(powers 5) * ↑(powers 7))

entries.name: erdos_123.variants.powers_2_3
entries.doc: A simpler case: the set of numbers of the form $2^k 3^l$ ($k, l ≥ 0$) is d-complete. This was initially conjectured by Erdős in 1992, who called it a "nice and difficult" problem, but it was quickly proven by Jansen and others using a simple inductive argument: - If $n = 2m$ is even, apply the inductive hypothesis to $m$ and double all summands. - If $n$ is odd, let $3^k$ be the largest power of $
entries.statement: IsDComplete (↑(powers 2) * ↑(powers 3))

entries.name: erdos_126.variants.IsBigO
entries.doc: Erdős and Turán proved [ErTu34] in their first joint paper that $$ \log n \ll f(n) \ll \frac{n}{\log n} $$ [ErTu34] Erdős, Paul and Turan, Paul, _On a Problem in the Elementary Theory of Numbers_. Amer. Math. Monthly (1934), 608-611.
entries.statement: ℕ → ℕ) (hf : IsMaximalAddFactorsCard f) : ((fun (n : ℕ) => Real.log n) =O[atTop] fun (n : ℕ) => (f n : ℝ)) ∧ (fun (n : ℕ) => (f n : ℝ)) =O[atTop] fun (n : ℕ) => n / Real.log n

entries.name: erdos_13
entries.doc: A finite set of naturals `A` is said to be forbidden-triple-free if for all `a, b, c ∈ A`, if `a < min(b, c)` then `a` does not divide `b + c`. -/ def IsForbiddenTripleFree (A : Finset ℕ) : Prop := ∀ a ∈ A, ∀ b ∈ A, ∀ c ∈ A, a < min b c → ¬ (a ∣ b + c) /-- If $A \subseteq \{1, ..., N\}$ is a set with no $a, b, c \in A$ such that $a | (b+c)$ and $a < \min(b,c)$, then $|A| \le N/3 + O(1)$. This has
entries.statement: ∃ C : ℝ, ∀ N : ℕ, ∀ A ⊆ Icc 1 N, IsForbiddenTripleFree A → (A.card : ℝ) ≤ (N : ℝ) / 3 + C

entries.name: monoAP_guarantee_set_nonempty
entries.doc: The set of natural numbers that guarantee a monochromatic arithmetic progression. A number `N` belongs to this set if, for a given number of colors `r` and an arithmetic progression length `k`, any `r`-coloring of the integers `{1, ..., N}` must contain a monochromatic arithmetic progression of length `k`. -/ def monoAP_guarantee_set (r k : ℕ) : Set ℕ := { N | ∀ coloring : Finset.Icc 1 N → Fin r,
entries.statement: (monoAP_guarantee_set r k).Nonempty

entries.name: erdos_138.variants.prime
entries.doc: When $p$ is prime Berlekamp [Be68] has proved $W(p+1) ≥ p^{2^p}$.
entries.statement: ℕ) (hp : p.Prime) : p * (2 ^ p) ≤ W (p + 1)

entries.name: erdos_138.variants.upper
entries.doc: Gowers [Go01] has proved $$W(k) \leq 2^{2^{2^{2^{2^{k+9}}}}.$$

entries.name: erdos_139
entries.doc: **Erdős Problem 139**: Let $r_k(N)$ be the size of the largest subset of ${1,...,N}$ which does not contain a non-trivial $k$-term arithmetic progression. Prove that $r_k(N) = o(N)$.
entries.statement: ℕ) (hk : 1 < k) : Filter.Tendsto (fun N => (r k N / N : ℝ)) Filter.atTop (𝓝 0)

entries.name: erdos_141.variants.first_cases
entries.doc: The existence of such progressions has been verified for $k≤10$.
entries.statement: (∀ k ≥ 3, k ≤ 10 → ∃ (s : Set ℕ), s.IsAPAndPrimeProgressionOfLength k)

entries.name: erdos_16.variant.romanoff
entries.doc: The set of odd integers not of the form $2^k+p$. -/ def Erdos16Set : Set ℕ := { n | Odd n ∧ ¬ ∃ k p : ℕ, p.Prime ∧ n = 2 ^ k + p } /-- A set of natural numbers has density 0. -/ def density_zero (S : Set ℕ) : Prop := Tendsto (fun x : ℕ ↦ (count S x : ℝ) / (x : ℝ)) atTop (𝓝 0) /-- Is the set of odd integers not of the form $2^k+p$ the union of an infinite arithmetic progression and a set of density

entries.name: erdos_18_upper_bound
entries.doc: **Erdős's Theorem.** Erdős proved that $h(n!) < n$ for all $n \ge 1$.
entries.statement: ∀ᶠ n : ℕ in atTop, practicalH (Nat.factorial n) < n

entries.name: erdos_18_vose
entries.doc: **Vose's Theorem.** Vose proved the existence of infinitely many practical numbers $m$ such that $h(m) \ll (\log m)^{1/2}$. This gives a positive answer to a weaker form of Conjecture 1.
entries.statement: ∃ C : ℝ, 0 < C ∧ ∃ᶠ m in atTop, Nat.IsPractical m ∧ (practicalH m : ℝ) < C * (log m) ^ (1 / 2 : ℝ)

entries.name: erdos_188.variants.nonempty
entries.doc: Old and new problems and results in combinatorial number theory by Erdős & Graham (Page 14, 15): It has been shown that there is a large $M$ so that it is possible to partition $\mathbb{E}^2$ into two sets $A$ and $B$ so that $A$ contains no pair of points with distance 1 and $B$ contains no A.P. of length $M$.
entries.statement: s.Nonempty

entries.name: erdos_188.variants.estimate
entries.doc: Old and new problems and results in combinatorial number theory by Erdős & Graham (Page 15): How small can $M$ be made? The only estimate currently known is that $M \le 10000000$ (more or less). In the other direction, it has just been shown by R. Juhász [Ju (79)] that we must have $M \ge 5$.

entries.name: theorem_1
entries.doc: Let $(S_n)_{n \ge 1}$ be a sequence of sets of complex numbers, none of which have a finite limit point. Does there exist an entire transcendental function $f(z)$ such that, for all $n \ge 1$, there exists some $k_n \ge 0$ such that $f^{(k_n)}(z) = 0$ for all $z \in S_n$. This is Problem 2.30 in [Ha74], where it is attributed to Erdős. Solved in the affirmative by Barth and Schneider [BaSc72]. Thi

entries.doc: A sequence $a_n$ of integers is called an irrationality sequence if for every bounded sequence of integers $b_n$ with $a_n + b_n \neq 0$ and $b_n \neq 0$ for all $n$, the sum $$ \sum \frac{1}{a_n + b_n} $$ is irrational.
