entries.name: erdos_323.variants.k_eq_2
entries.doc: The case $k=2$ was resolved by Landau, who showed $f_{2,2}(x) \sim \frac{cx}{\sqrt{\log x}}$ for some constant $c>0$.
entries.statement: ∃ c > 0, (fun (x : ℕ) ↦ (f 2 2 x : ℝ)) ~[atTop] (fun (x : ℕ) ↦ c * (x : ℝ) / Real.sqrt (Real.log (x : ℝ)))

entries.name: erdos_325.variants.wooley
entries.doc: For $k = 3$, the best known is due to Wooley [Wo15] [Wo15] Wooley, Trevor D., Sums of three cubes, II. Acta Arith. (2015), 73-100.
entries.statement: (fun x : ℕ => (x : ℝ) ^ (0.917 : ℝ)) =O[atTop] (fun x => (cardIsSumThreePowerBelow 3 x : ℝ))

entries.name: erdos_329.variants.lower_bound
entries.doc: Erdős proved that upper density `1 / 2` can be attained; in particular, there exists a Sidon set whose upper density is *at least* `1 / 2`.
entries.statement: ∃ (A : Set ℕ), IsSidon A ∧ sidonUpperDensity A ≥ 1/2

entries.name: erdos_329.variants.kruckeberg_1961
entries.doc: Krückeberg ([Kr61]) exhibited an infinite Sidon set `A` with `sidonUpperDensity A = 1 / Real.sqrt 2`, improving Erdős’ earlier `1 / 2` lower bound. [Kr61] Krückeberg, Fritz, $B\sb{2}$-Folgen und verwandte Zahlenfolgen. J. Reine Angew. Math. (1961), 53-60.
entries.statement: ∃ (A : Set ℕ), IsSidon A ∧ sidonUpperDensity A = 1 / Real.sqrt 2

entries.name: erdos_329.variants.turan_1941
entries.doc: Erdős and Turán [ErTu41] proved the upper bound of 1. [ErTu41] Erdős, P. and Turán, P., On a problem of Sidon in additive number theory, and on some related problems. J. London Math. Soc. (1941), 212-215.
entries.statement: ∀ (A : Set ℕ), IsSidon A → sidonUpperDensity A ≤ 1

entries.name: erdos_33.variants.one_mem_lowerBounds
entries.doc: Erdos observed that this value is finite and > 1.
entries.statement: ∃ A, AdditiveBasisCondition A ∧ 1 < Filter.atTop.limsup (fun N => (A ∩ Icc 1 N).ncard / √N)

entries.name: erdos_33.variants.vanDoorn
entries.doc: The smallest possible value of `lim sup n → ∞ |A ∩ {1, …, N}| / N^(1/2)` is at most `2φ^(5/2) ≈ 6.66`, with `φ` equal to the golden ratio. Proven by Wouter van Doorn.
entries.statement: ⨅ A : {A : Set ℕ | AdditiveBasisCondition A}, Filter.atTop.limsup (fun N => (A.1 ∩ Icc 1 N).ncard / (√N : EReal)) ≤ ↑(2 * (φ ^ ((5 : ℝ) / 2)))

entries.name: erdos_340.variants._22_mem_sub
entries.doc: Erdős and Graham [ErGr80] also asked about the difference set $A - A$ and whether this contains $22$, which it does. [ErGr80] Erdős, P. and Graham, R., Old and new problems and results in combinatorial number theory. Monographies de L'Enseignement Mathematique (1980).
entries.statement: 22 ∈ Set.range greedySidon - Set.range greedySidon

entries.name: erdos_346.variants.f_isAddStronglyCompleteNatSeq
entries.doc: We define a sequence `f` by the formula `f n = n.fib - (- 1) ^ n`. -/ def f (n : ℕ) : ℕ := if Even n then n.fib - 1 else n.fib + 1 /-- The sequence `f` is lacunary. -/ @[category test, AMS 11] theorem erdos_346.variants.f_isLacunary : IsLacunary f := by refine ⟨3/2, by norm_num, Filter.eventually_atTop.mpr ⟨9, fun k hk => ?_⟩⟩ -- Key: `2·fib(k+1) > 3·fib(k) + 5` for `k ≥ 9`, since -- `2·fib(k+1) -
entries.statement: IsAddStronglyCompleteNatSeq f

entries.name: erdos_346.variants.f_not_isAddComplete
entries.doc: The sequence `f` is not complete whenever infinitely many terms are removed from it, and this is proved in [Gr64d].
entries.statement: Set ℕ} (h : B ⊆ range f) (hB : B.Infinite) : ¬ IsAddComplete (range f \ B)

entries.name: erdos_350.variants.strengthening
entries.doc: The predicate that all (finite) subsets of `A` have distinct sums. -/ def DistinctSubsetSums {M : Type*} [AddCommMonoid M] (A : Set M) : Prop := Set.Pairwise {X : Finset M | ↑X ⊆ A} fun X Y => X.sum id ≠ Y.sum id /-- The predicate that all (finite) subsets of `A` have distinct sums, decidable version -/ def DecidableDistinctSubsetSums {M : Type*} [AddCommMonoid M] [DecidableEq M] (A : Finset M) :
entries.statement: Finset ℕ) (hA : DecidableDistinctSubsetSums A) (s : ℝ) (hs : 0 < s) : ∑ n ∈ A, (1 / n : ℝ)^s < 1 / (1 - 2^(-s))

entries.name: erdos_351.variants.X_sq
entries.doc: Let $p(x) = x \in \mathbb{Q}[x]$. It has been shown that $$A=\{ p(n)+1/n : n \in \mathbb{N}\}$$ is strongly complete, in the sense that, for any finite set $B$, $$\left\{\sum_{a \in X} a : X \subseteq A \setminus B, X \textrm{ is finite}\right\}$$ contains all sufficiently large integers. -/ @[category research solved, AMS 11] protected theorem erdos_351.variants.X : HasCompleteImage X := by sorry
entries.statement: HasCompleteImage (X ^ 2)

entries.name: erdos_357.variants.weisenberg
entries.doc: Let $f(n)$ be the maximal $k$ such that there exist integers $1 \le a_1 < \dotsc < a_k \le n$ such that all sums of the shape $\sum_{u \le i \le v} a_i$ are distinct. It is known that $f(n) \geq (2+o(1))\sqrt{n}$. Source: See comment by Desmond Weisenberg here: https://www.erdosproblems.com/forum/thread/357.
entries.statement: ∃ o : ℕ → ℝ, o =o[atTop] (1 : ℕ → ℝ) ∧ ∀ᶠ n in atTop, (2 + o n) * √n ≤ f n

entries.name: erdos_357.variants.infinite_set_lower_density
entries.doc: Suppose $A$ is an infinite set such that all finite sums of consecutive terms of $A$ are distinct. Then $A$ has lower density 0.
entries.statement: ℕ → ℕ) (hA : StrictMono A) (hA : HasDistinctSums A) : (Set.range A).lowerDensity = 0

entries.name: erdos_358.parts.i
entries.doc: When $A_n = n$, the function $f$ defined above counts the number of odd divisors of $n$. -/ @[category textbook, AMS 5 11] theorem f_id : f id = fun n ↦ #{d ∈ n.divisors | Odd d} := by sorry /-- Let $A=\{a_1 < \cdots\}$ be an infinite sequence of integers. Let $f(n)$ count the number of solutions to $$n=\sum_{u\leq i\leq v}a_i.$$ Is there such an $A$ for which $f(n)\to \infty$ as $n\to \infty$? Ta
entries.statement: answer(True) ↔ ∃ A, StrictMono A ∧ atTop.Tendsto (f A) atTop

entries.name: erdos_358.parts.ii
entries.doc: Let $A=\{a_1 < \cdots\}$ be an infinite sequence of integers. Let $f(n)$ count the number of solutions to $$n=\sum_{u\leq i\leq v}a_i.$$ Is there an $A$ such that $f(n)\geq 2$ for all large $n$? This also follows from Tao's construction with $f(n) \gg \log n$ [Ta26].
entries.statement: answer(True) ↔ ∃ A, StrictMono A ∧ ∀ᶠ n in atTop, 2 ≤ f A n

entries.name: minimum_overlap.variants.lower.scherk_1955
entries.doc: The number of solutions to the equation $a - b = k$, for $a \in A$ and $b \in B$. This represents the "overlap" between sets $A$ and $B$ for a given difference $k$. -/ def Overlap (A B : Finset ℤ) (k : ℤ) : ℕ := ((A.product B).filter <| fun (a, b) => a - b = k).card /-- The maximum overlap for a given pair of sets $A$ and $B$, taken over all possible integer differences $k$. -/ noncomputable def M
entries.statement: 1 - (√2)⁻¹ < atTop.liminf MinOverlapQuotient

entries.name: minimum_overlap.variants.lower.swierczkowski_1958
entries.doc: A lower bound of $\frac{4 - \sqrt{6}}{5}. See [On the intersection of a linear set with the translation of its complement](https://bibliotekanauki.pl/articles/969027) by *Stanisław Świerczkowski1*, Colloquium Mathematicum 5(2), p. 185-197, 1958
entries.statement: (4 - 6 ^ ((1 : ℝ) / 2)) / 5 < atTop.liminf MinOverlapQuotient

entries.name: minimum_overlap.variants.lower.haugland_1996
entries.doc: A lower bound of $\sqrt{4 - \sqrt{15}}$.
entries.statement: (4 - 15 ^((1 : ℝ) / 2)) ^ ((1 : ℝ) / 2) < atTop.liminf MinOverlapQuotient

entries.name: minimum_overlap.variants.lower.white_2022
entries.doc: A lower bound of $0.379005$. See [Erdős' minimum overlap problem](https://arxiv.org/abs/2201.05704) by *Ethan Patrick White*, 2022
entries.statement: 0.379005 < atTop.liminf MinOverlapQuotient

entries.name: minimum_overlap.variants.upper.erdos_1955
entries.doc: The example (with $N$ even), $A = \{\frac N 2 + 1, \dots, \frac{3N}{2}\}$ shows an upper bound of $\frac 1 2$.
entries.statement: atTop.limsup MinOverlapQuotient ≤ (1 : ℝ) / 2

entries.name: minimum_overlap.variants.upper.MRS_1956
entries.doc: An upper bound of $\frac 2 5$. See [Minimal overlapping under translation.](https://projecteuclid.org/journals/bulletin-of-the-american-mathematical-society/volume-62/issue-6) by *T. S. Motzkin*, *K. E. Ralston* and *J. L. Selfridge*, in "The summer meeting in Seattle" by *V. L. Klee Jr.*, Bull. Amer. Math. Soc.62, p. 558, 1956
entries.statement: atTop.limsup MinOverlapQuotient ≤ (2 : ℝ) / 5

entries.name: minimum_overlap.variants.upper.haugland_1996
entries.doc: An upper bound of $0.38200298812318988$. See [Advances in the Minimum Overlap Problem](https://doi.org/10.1006%2Fjnth.1996.0064) by *Jan Kristian Haugland*, Journal of Number Theory Volume 58, Issue 1, p 71-78, 1996
entries.statement: atTop.limsup MinOverlapQuotient ≤ 0.38200298812318988

entries.name: minimum_overlap.variants.upper.haugland_2022
entries.doc: An upper bound of $0.3809268534330870$. See [The minimum overlap problem](https://www.neutreeko.net/mop/index.htm) by *Jan Kristian Haugland*
entries.statement: atTop.limsup MinOverlapQuotient ≤ 0.3809268534330870

entries.name: erdos_36.variants.exists
entries.doc: The limit of `MinOverlapQuotient` exists and it is less than $0.385694$.
entries.statement: ∃ c, atTop.Tendsto MinOverlapQuotient (𝓝 c) ∧ c < 0.385694

entries.name: erdos_373.variants.of_limit
entries.doc: Show that if `P(n(n+1)) / log n → ∞` where `P(m)` denotes the largest prime factor of `m`, then the equation `n!=a_1!a_2!···a_k!`, with `n−1 > a_1 ≥ a_2 ≥ ··· ≥ a_k`, has only finitely many solutions.
entries.statement: Filter.atTop.Tendsto (fun (n : ℕ) => (n*(n+1)).maxPrimeFac / (n : ℝ).log) Filter.atTop) : S.Finite

entries.name: erdos_373.variants.of_lower_bound
entries.doc: Show that if `P(n(n−1)) > 4 log n` for large enough `n`, where `P(m)` denotes the largest prime factor of `m`, then the equation `n!=a_1!a_2!···a_k!`, with `n−1 > a_1 ≥ a_2 ≥ ··· ≥ a_k`, has only finitely many solutions.
entries.statement: ∀ᶠ (n : ℕ) in Filter.atTop, 4*(n : ℝ).log < (n*(n-1 : ℕ)).maxPrimeFac) : S.Finite

entries.name: erdos_375.variants.bounded_gap
entries.doc: If `Erdos375Prop` is true, then `(n + 1).nth Prime - n.nth Prime < (n.nth Prime) ^ (1 / 2 - c)` for some `c > 0`.
entries.statement: Erdos375Prop → ∃ c > 0, ∀ᶠ n in atTop, (n + 1).nth Nat.Prime - n.nth Nat.Prime < (n.nth Nat.Prime : ℝ) ^ (1 / (2 : ℝ) - c)

entries.name: erdos_377.variants.limit.i
entries.doc: Erdos, Graham, Ruzsa, and Straus proved that if $$ f(n) = \sum_{p \leq n} 1_{p\nmid {2n \choose n}}\frac{1}{p} $$ and $$ \gamma_0 = \sum_{k = 2}^{\infty} \frac{\log k}{2^k} $$ then $$ \lim_{x\to\infty} \frac{1}{x}\sum_{n\leq x} f(n) = \gamma_0 $$ [EGRS75] Erdős, P. and Graham, R. L. and Ruzsa, I. Z. and Straus, E. G., _On the prime factors of $\binom{2n}{n}$_. Math. Comp. (1975), 83-92.
entries.statement: ℝ) (hγ₀ : γ₀ = ∑' (k : ℕ), (k + 2 : ℝ).log / 2 ^ (k + 2)) : Tendsto (fun (x : ℕ) => (1 : ℝ) / x * ∑ n ∈ Finset.Icc 1 x, sumInvPrimesNotDvdCentralBinom n) atTop (𝓝 γ₀)

entries.name: erdos_377.variants.limit.ii
entries.doc: Erdos, Graham, Ruzsa, and Straus proved that if $$ f(n) = \sum_{p \leq n} 1_{p\nmid {2n \choose n}}\frac{1}{p} $$ and $$ \gamma_0 = \sum_{k = 2}^{\infty} \frac{\log k}{2^k} $$ then $$ \lim_{x\to\infty} \frac{1}{x}\sum_{n\leq x} f(n)^2 = \gamma_0^2 $$ [EGRS75] Erdős, P. and Graham, R. L. and Ruzsa, I. Z. and Straus, E. G., _On the prime factors of $\binom{2n}{n}$_. Math. Comp. (1975), 83-92.
entries.statement: ℝ) (hγ₀ : γ₀ = ∑' (k : ℕ), (k + 2 : ℝ).log / 2 ^ (k + 2)) : Tendsto (fun (x : ℕ) => (1 : ℝ) / x * ∑ n ∈ Finset.Icc 1 x, sumInvPrimesNotDvdCentralBinom n ^ 2) atTop (𝓝 (γ₀ ^ 2))

entries.name: erdos_377.variants.ae
entries.doc: Erdos, Graham, Ruzsa, and Straus proved that if $$ f(n) = \sum_{p \leq n} 1_{p\nmid {2n \choose n}}\frac{1}{p} $$ and $$ \gamma_0 = \sum_{k = 2}^{\infty} \frac{\log k}{2^k} $$ then for almost all integers $f(m) = \gamma_0 + o(1)$. [EGRS75] Erdős, P. and Graham, R. L. and Ruzsa, I. Z. and Straus, E. G., _On the prime factors of $\binom{2n}{n}$_. Math. Comp. (1975), 83-92.
entries.statement: ℝ) (hγ₀ : γ₀ = ∑' (k : ℕ), (k + 2 : ℝ).log / 2 ^ (k + 2)) : ∃ (o : ℕ → ℝ) (_ : Tendsto o atTop (𝓝 0)), ∀ᶠ n in cofinite, sumInvPrimesNotDvdCentralBinom n = γ₀ + o n

entries.name: erdos_377.variants.ub
entries.doc: Erdos, Graham, Ruzsa, and Straus proved that if $$ f(n) = \sum_{p \leq n} 1_{p\nmid {2n \choose n}}\frac{1}{p} $$ then there is some constant $c < 1$ such that for all large $n$ $$ f(n) \leq c \log\log n. $$ [EGRS75] Erdős, P. and Graham, R. L. and Ruzsa, I. Z. and Straus, E. G., _On the prime factors of $\binom{2n}{n}$_. Math. Comp. (1975), 83-92.
entries.statement: ∃ c < (1 : ℝ), ∀ᶠ n in atTop, sumInvPrimesNotDvdCentralBinom n ≤ c * (n : ℝ).log.log

entries.name: erdos_390.variants.theta
entries.doc: Let `f n` be the smallest integer for which `n!` can be represented as the product of distinct integers greater than n, the largest of which is `f n`. -/ noncomputable def f (n : ℕ) : ℕ := sInf {m : ℕ | ∃ k, ∃ f : ℕ → ℕ, StrictMono f ∧ n < f 0 ∧ f (k - 1) = m ∧ ∏ i < k, f i = n !} /-- `f n - 2 * n = θ (n / log n)`. This is proved in [EGS82].
entries.statement: (fun n => f n - 2 * n : ℕ → ℝ) =Θ[atTop] (fun n => n / log (n : ℝ))

entries.name: erdos_394.variants.hall_bound
entries.doc: In [ErGr80] they mention a conjecture of Erdős that the sum is $o(x^2)$. This was proved by Erdős and Hall [ErHa78], who proved that in fact $\sum_{n\leq x}t_2(n)\ll \frac{\log\log\log x}{\log\log x}x^2.$
entries.statement: (fun x ↦ ∑ n ∈ Icc 1 ⌊x⌋₊, (t 2 n : ℝ)) ≪ (fun x ↦ x ^ 2 * (Real.log (Real.log (Real.log x)) / Real.log (Real.log x)))

entries.name: erdos_394.variants.lower_bound
entries.doc: Since $t_2(p)=p-1$ for prime $p$ it is trivial that $\sum_{n\leq x}t_2(n)\gg \frac{x^2}{\log x}$.
entries.statement: (fun x ↦ x ^ 2 / Real.log x) ≫ (fun x ↦ ∑ n ∈ Icc 1 ⌊x⌋₊, (t 2 n : ℝ))

entries.name: erdos_394.variants.factorial_gap_10
entries.doc: They proved (with Selfridge) that this holds for $n=10$.

entries.name: erdos_4
entries.doc: Is it true that, for any $C > 0$, there infinitely many $n$ such that: $$ p_{n + 1} - p_n > C \frac{\log\log n\log\log\log\log n}{(\log\log\log n) ^ 2}\log n $$
entries.statement: answer(True) ↔ (∀ C > 0, Erdos4For C)

entries.name: erdos_4.variants.rankin
entries.doc: Rankin's theorem: there exists a positive constant $C$ such that `Erdos4For C` holds.
entries.statement: ∃ C > 0, Erdos4For C

entries.name: erdos_400.variants.upper_bound
entries.doc: Erdős and Graham write that it is easy to show that $g_k(n) \ll_k \log n$ always, but the best possible constant is unknown.
entries.statement: ℕ) (hk : k ≥ 2) : (fun n : ℕ ↦ (g k n : ℝ)) ≪ (fun n : ℕ ↦ Real.log (n : ℝ))

entries.name: erdos_41.variants.pairwise
entries.doc: Erdős proved the following pairwise version. Let `A ⊆ ℕ` be an infinite set such that the pairwise sums `a + b` are all distinct for `a, b` in `A` (aside from the trivial coincidences). Is it true that `liminf n → ∞ |A ∩ {1, …, N}| / N^(1/2) = 0`?
entries.statement: Set ℕ) (hA₂ : NtupleCondition A 2) (hA : A.Infinite) : Filter.atTop.liminf (fun N => (A ∩ Icc 1 N).ncard / (N : ℝ).sqrt) = 0

entries.name: erdos_413.variants.hasPosDensity_barrier_expProd
entries.doc: `expProd n` is `∏ kᵢ` when `n = ∏ pᵢ ^ kᵢ`, i.e. the product of the prime exponents of `n`. -/ def expProd (n : ℕ) : ℕ := n.factorization.prod fun _ e => e /-- Erdős proved that the barrier set for `expProd` is infinite and even has positive density.
entries.statement: { n | IsBarrier (fun m => expProd m) n }.HasPosDensity

entries.name: erdos_413.variants.bigOmega_largest_barrier_lt_100k
entries.doc: Selfridge computed that the largest `Ω`-barrier below `10^5` is `99840`.
entries.statement: IsGreatest {n : ℕ | n < 10 ^ 5 ∧ IsBarrier (fun m => Ω m) n} 99840

entries.name: erdos_416.variants.Pillai
entries.doc: Let `V(x)` count the number of `n≤x` such that `ϕ(m)=n` is solvable. Pillai proved `V(x)=o(x)`. Ref: S. Sivasankaranarayana Pillai, _On some functions connected with $\phi(n)$_
entries.statement: V =o[atTop] id

entries.name: erdos_416.variants.Erdos
entries.doc: Let `V(x)` count the number of `n≤x` such that `ϕ(m)=n` is solvable. Erdős proved V(x)=x(logx)^(−1+o(1)). Ref: Erdős, P., _On the normal number of prime factors of $p-1$ and some related problems concerning Euler's $\varphi$-function._
entries.statement: ∃ f : ℝ → ℝ, f =o[atTop] (1 : ℝ → ℝ) ∧ ∀ᶠ x in Filter.atTop, V x = x * x.log ^ (-1 + f x)

entries.name: erdos_427.variants.shiu
entries.doc: The predicate that for every $n$ and $d$, there exists $k$ such that $$ d \mid p_{n + 1} + \cdots + p_{n + k}, $$ where $p_r$ denotes the $r$th prime? -/ def erdos427 : Prop := ∀ (n d : ℕ), -- Need to allow `n = 0` since we're counting primes from `0` rather than `1` -- `d` needs to be `≠ 0` since the sum is never `0`! d ≠ 0 → ∃ k, k ≠ 0 ∧ d ∣ ∑ i ∈ Finset.Ico n (n + k), i.nth Nat.Prime /-- **Erdő
entries.statement: ShiuTheorem

entries.name: erdos_427.variants.of_shiu
entries.doc: Cedric Pilatte has observed that a positive solution to Erdős Problem 427 follows from Shiu's theorem.
entries.statement: ShiuTheorem) : erdos427

entries.name: erdos_445.variants.heilbronn
entries.doc: Heilbronn (unpublished) proved this for $c$ sufficiently close to $1$.
entries.statement: ∃ c₀ < 1, ∀ c : ℝ, c > c₀ → ∀ᶠ p : ℕ in atTop, p.Prime → ∀ n : ℕ, Erdos445Prop c p n

entries.name: erdos_445.variants.heath_brown
entries.doc: Heath-Brown [He00] used Kloosterman sums to prove this for all $c>3/4$.
entries.statement: ∀ c : ℝ, c > 3 / 4 → ∀ᶠ p : ℕ in atTop, p.Prime → ∀ n : ℕ, Erdos445Prop c p n

entries.name: erdos_454.variants.two_le_limsup
entries.doc: `limsup (fun n => (f n - 2 * n.nth Prime : ℕ∞)) atTop ≥ 2`, and this is proved in [Po79].
entries.statement: 2 ≤ limsup (fun n => (f n - 2 * n.nth Prime : ℕ∞)) atTop

entries.name: erdos_456.variants.linniks_theorem
entries.doc: Linnik's theorem implies that $p_n\leq n^{O(1)}$.
entries.statement: ∃ L : ℝ, (fun n ↦ (p n : ℝ)) =O[atTop] (fun n ↦ (n : ℝ) ^ L)

entries.name: erdos_456.variants.infinitely_many_n
entries.doc: It is trivial that $m_n \leq p_n$ always. -/ @[category textbook, AMS 11] theorem erdos_456.variants.mn_leq_pn (n : ℕ) : m n ≤ p n := by sorry /-- Erdős [Er79e] writes it is 'easy to show' that for infinitely many $n$ we have $m_n < p_n$.
entries.statement: { n | m n < p n }.Infinite

entries.name: erdos_456.variants.m_div_n
entries.doc: Erdős [Er79e] writes it is 'easy to show' that $m_n/n \to \infty$ for almost all $n$.
entries.statement: ∃ A : Set ℕ, Tendsto (fun N ↦ (count A N : ℝ) / N) atTop (𝓝 1) ∧ Tendsto (fun n ↦ (m n : ℝ) / (n : ℝ)) (atTop ⊓ 𝓟 A) atTop

entries.name: erdos_470.variants.prime_gap_imp_inf_prim_weird
entries.doc: The smallest weird number is 70. -/ @[category textbook, AMS 11] theorem erdos_470.variants.smallest_weird_eq_70 : (∀ n < 70, ¬n.Weird) ∧ (70).Weird := by refine ⟨?_, Nat.weird_seventy⟩ rintro n hn ⟨ha, hnp⟩ unfold Nat.Abundant at ha apply hnp -- For non-abundant `n`, `ha` is contradictory; for each abundant `n < 70`, exhibit an explicit -- subset of its proper divisors summing to `n` (so `n` is p
entries.statement: ∀ᶠ n in Filter.atTop, primeGap n < √ (n.nth Nat.Prime) / 10 → Set.Infinite PrimitiveWeird

entries.name: erdos_470.variants.abundancy_index
entries.doc: If there are no odd weird numbers then every weird number has abundancy index < 4.
entries.statement: (∀ n : ℕ, n.Weird → ¬Odd n) → ∀ n, n.Weird → AbundancyIndex n < 4

entries.name: erdos_489.variants.squarefree
entries.doc: When $A = \{p^2 : p \textrm{ prime}\}$, $B$ is the set of squarefree numbers, and the existence of this limit was proved by Erdős. This is the $\alpha = 2$ case of Erdős Problem 145.
entries.statement: ∃ L : ℝ, Tendsto (fun x : ℕ => GapSumSq {n | ∃ p, Nat.Prime p ∧ n = p ^ 2} x / (x : ℝ)) atTop (𝓝 L)

entries.name: erdos_494.variants.k_eq_2_card_not_pow_two
entries.doc: For a finite set $A \subset \mathbb{C}$ and $k \ge 1$, define $A_k$ as the multiset consisting of all sums of $k$ distinct elements of $A$. -/ noncomputable def sumMultiset (A : Finset ℂ) (k : ℕ) : Multiset ℂ := (A.powersetCard k).val.map fun s => s.sum id def Erdos494Unique (k : ℕ) (card : ℕ) := ∀ A B : Finset ℂ, A.card = card → B.card = card → sumMultiset A k = sumMultiset B k → A = B /-- Selfri
entries.statement: ∀ card : ℕ, (∀ l : ℕ, card ≠ 2 ^ l) → Erdos494Unique 2 card

entries.name: erdos_494.variants.k_eq_2_card_pow_two
entries.doc: Selfridge and Straus [SeSt58] gave counterexamples to the conjecture when $k = 2$ and $|A| = 2^l$.
entries.statement: ∀ card : ℕ, (∃ l : ℕ, card = 2 ^ l) → ¬Erdos494Unique 2 card

entries.name: erdos_494.variants.k_eq_3_card_gt_6
entries.doc: Selfridge and Straus [SeSt58] also showed that the conjecture is true when 1) $k = 3$ and $|A| > 6$ or 2) $k = 4$ and $|A| > 12$. More generally, they proved that $A$ is determined by $A_k$ (and $|A|$) if $|A|$ is divisible by a prime greater than $k$.
entries.statement: ∀ card > 6, Erdos494Unique 3 card

entries.name: erdos_494.variants.k_eq_4_card_gt_12
entries.doc: Selfridge and Straus [SeSt58] showed that the conjecture is true when $k = 4$ and $|A| > 12$.
entries.statement: ∀ card > 12, Erdos494Unique 4 card

entries.name: erdos_494.variants.card_divisible_by_prime_gt_k
entries.doc: Selfridge and Straus [SeSt58] proved that $A$ is determined by $A_k$ if $|A|$ is divisible by a prime greater than $k$.
entries.statement: ∀ (k card p : ℕ), p.Prime → k ∈ Set.Ioo 0 p → p ∣ card → Erdos494Unique k card

entries.name: erdos_494.variants.k_eq_card
entries.doc: Kruyt noted that the conjecture fails when $|A| = k$, by rotating $A$ around an appropriate point.
entries.statement: ∀ k > 2, ¬Erdos494Unique k k

entries.name: erdos_494.variants.card_eq_2k
entries.doc: Similarly, Tao noted that the conjecture fails when $|A| = 2k$, by taking $A$ to be a set of the total sum 0 and considering $-A$.
entries.statement: ∀ k > 2, ¬Erdos494Unique k (2 * k)

entries.name: erdos_494.variants.gordon_fraenkel_straus
entries.doc: Gordon, Fraenkel, and Straus [GRS62] proved that the claim is true for all $k > 2$ when $|A|$ is sufficiently large.
entries.statement: ∀ k > 2, ∀ᶠ card in atTop, Erdos494Unique k card

entries.name: erdos_494.variants.product
entries.doc: A version in [Er61] by Erdős is product instead of sum, which is false. Counterexample (by Steinerberger): consider $k = 3$ and let $A = \{1, \zeta_6, \zeta_6^2, \zeta_6^4\}$ and $B = \{1, \zeta_6^2, \zeta_6^3, \zeta_6^4\}$. -/ noncomputable def prodMultiset (A : Finset ℂ) (k : ℕ) : Multiset ℂ := ((A.powersetCard k).val.map (fun s => s.prod id)) /-- A counterexample to the product version of the c
entries.statement: ∃ (A B : Finset ℂ), A.card = B.card ∧ prodMultiset A 3 = prodMultiset B 3 ∧ A ≠ B

entries.name: erdos_50_schoenberg
entries.doc: A function $f : \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ is the asymptotic distribution function of the values of $\varphi(n)/n$ if for all $c \in [0, 1]$, the natural density of $\{n : \varphi(n) < cn\}$ exists and equals $f(c)$. -/ def IsDistributionOfPhiRatio (f : ℝ → ℝ) : Prop := ∀ c ∈ Icc (0 : ℝ) 1, {n : ℕ | (φ n : ℝ) < c * n}.HasDensity (f c) /-- A monotone function $f : \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ is pure
entries.statement: ∃ f : ℝ → ℝ, IsDistributionOfPhiRatio f

entries.name: erdos_50_singular
entries.doc: Erdős [Er95] proved that the distribution function of $\varphi(n)/n$ is purely singular: it is continuous, but its derivative is zero almost everywhere.
entries.statement: ℝ → ℝ) (hf : IsDistributionOfPhiRatio f) : IsPurelySingular f

entries.name: erdos_535.variants.erdos_upper_bound
entries.doc: Erdős [Er64] proved that $f_r(N) \leq N^{3/4+o(1)}$ for all $r \geq 3$.
entries.statement: ℕ} (hr : 3 ≤ r) : ∀ ε > (0 : ℝ), ∀ᶠ (N : ℕ) in atTop, (f r N : ℝ) ≤ (N : ℝ) ^ ((3 : ℝ) / 4 + ε)

entries.name: erdos_535.variants.lower_bound
entries.doc: Erdős [Er64] proved that $f_3(N) > N^{c/\log\log N}$ for some constant $c > 0$.
entries.statement: ∃ c > (0 : ℝ), ∀ᶠ (N : ℕ) in atTop, (N : ℝ) ^ (c / log (log (N : ℝ))) ≤ (f 3 N : ℝ)

entries.name: erdos_535.variants.abbott_hanson
entries.doc: Abbott and Hanson [AbHa70] improved Erdős's upper bound to $f_r(N) \leq N^{1/2+o(1)}$ for all $r \geq 3$.
entries.statement: ℕ} (hr : 3 ≤ r) : ∀ ε > (0 : ℝ), ∀ᶠ (N : ℕ) in atTop, (f r N : ℝ) ≤ (N : ℝ) ^ ((1 : ℝ) / 2 + ε)

entries.name: erdos_535.variants.sunflower_erdos_rado
entries.doc: For the stronger $Ω(n)=k$ variant above, the Erdős–Rado method gives the weaker bound $c_r^k \cdot k!$; see Erdős [Er73].
entries.statement: ℕ} (hr : 3 ≤ r) : ∃ c_r > (0 : ℝ), ∀ k : ℕ, ∀ A : Finset ℕ, AllBigOmega k A → NoConstantPairwiseGcdCoprimeSubsets r A → (A.card : ℝ) ≤ c_r ^ k * (Nat.factorial k)

entries.name: erdos_539.variants.sq_isBigO
entries.doc: Erdős and Szemerédi proved that$$n^{1/2} \ll h(n)$$.
entries.statement: (fun n : ℕ ↦ √n) =O[atTop] fun n ↦ (cofactorThreshold n : ℝ)

entries.name: erdos_539.variants.isBigO_sq_cube_root
entries.doc: Granville and Roesler [GR99] showed that $$h(n)\ll n^{2/3}$$.
entries.statement: (fun n ↦ (cofactorThreshold n : ℝ)) =O[atTop] fun n ↦ (n : ℝ) ^ ((2 : ℝ) / 3)

entries.name: erdos_587.variants.nguyen_vu
entries.doc: `MaxNotSqSum N` is the size of the largest subset `A` of `{1,...,N}` such that for all non-empty `S ⊆ A`, the sum `∑ n ∈ S, n` is not a square. -/ def MaxNotSqSum (N : ℕ) : ℕ := (Finset.Icc 1 N |>.powerset.filter fun A => ∀ S ⊆ A, S ≠ ⊥ → ¬ IsSquare (∑ n ∈ S, n)).sup Finset.card /-- Nguyen and Vu proved that $|A| \ll N^{1/3} (\log N)^{O(1)}$.
entries.statement: ∃ᵉ (O > 0) (O' > 0), ∀ᶠ N in Filter.atTop, (MaxNotSqSum N : ℝ) ≤ O' * Real.nthRoot 3 N * (N : ℝ).log^O

entries.name: erdos_602.variants.disjoint
entries.doc: **Trivial case: pairwise disjoint families.** If the `A_i` are pairwise disjoint (all intersections are empty, which in particular satisfies `|A_i ∩ A_j| ≠ 1`), then Property B holds trivially. **Proof sketch:** Since each `A_i` is infinite, it has (at least) two distinct elements `a_i` and `b_i`. We can define a colouring that assigns colour 0 to `a_i` and colour 1 to `b_i` for each `i` (using di
entries.statement: answer(True) ↔ ∀ {α : Type*} {I : Type*} (A : I → Set α), (∀ i, (A i).Infinite) → (∀ i j, i ≠ j → Disjoint (A i) (A j)) → HasPropertyB I A

entries.name: erdos_602.variants.countable_index
entries.doc: **Countable index set case.** If the index set is countable, the answer is yes, and the intersection condition is unnecessary. This is Bernstein's Lemma: every countable system of infinite sets has Property B.
entries.statement: answer(True) ↔ ∀ {α : Type*} (A : ℕ → Set α), (∀ i, (A i).Countable ∧ (A i).Infinite) → (∀ i j, i ≠ j → (A i ∩ A j).Finite) → (∀ i j, i ≠ j → Set.ncard (A i ∩ A j) ≠ 1) → HasPropertyB ℕ A

entries.name: erdos_602.variants.two_sets
entries.doc: **Intersections of size ≥ 2 suffice.** For a single countably infinite set `A ⊆ α`, there trivially exists a 2-colouring of `α` that makes `A` non-monochromatic: since `A` is infinite, it has two distinct elements, so any colouring that assigns them different colours works. -/ @[category textbook, AMS 3] theorem erdos_602.variants.single_set {α : Type*} (A : Set α) (hA : A.Infinite) : ∃ f : α → Fi
entries.statement: answer(True) ↔ ∀ {α : Type*} (A : Fin 2 → Set α), (∀ i, (A i).Infinite) → (A 0 ∩ A 1).Finite → Set.ncard (A 0 ∩ A 1) ≠ 1 → HasPropertyB (Fin 2) A

entries.name: erdos_61.variants.erha89
entries.doc: Erdős and Hajnal [ErHa89] proved that we can take $f(n) = \exp(c_H \sqrt{\log n})$ for some constant $c_H > 0$ dependending on $H$. [ErHa89] Erdős, P. and Hajnal, A., Ramsey-type theorems. Discrete Appl. Math. (1989), 37-52.
entries.statement: ∀ {α : Type*} [Fintype α] [DecidableEq α] (H : SimpleGraph α), ∃ c > (0 : ℝ), IsErdosHajnalLowerBound H (fun n : ℕ => exp (c * sqrt (log n)))

entries.name: erdos_61.variants.bnss23
entries.doc: Bucić, Nguyen, Scott, and Seymour [BNSS23] improved this to $f(n) = \exp(c_H \sqrt{\log n \log \log n})$ for some constant $c_H > 0$ dependending on $H$. [BNSS23] Bucić, M. and Nguyen, T. and Scott, A. and Seymour, P., A loglog step towards Erdos-Hajnal
entries.statement: ∀ {α : Type*} [Fintype α] [DecidableEq α] (H : SimpleGraph α), ∃ c > (0 : ℝ), IsErdosHajnalLowerBound H (fun n : ℕ => exp (c * sqrt (log n * log (log n))))

entries.name: erdos_683.variant.sylvester_schur
entries.doc: Sylvester and Schur [Er34] proved that $P(n, k) > k$ for $k \le n/2$.
entries.statement: ∀ n k : ℕ, 0 < k ∧ k ≤ n / 2 → P n k > k

entries.name: erdos_683.variant.erdos_log
entries.doc: Erdos [Er55d] improved this to $P(n, k) \gg k \log k $ for $k \le n/2$.
entries.statement: ∃ c > 0, ∀ n k : ℕ, 0 < k ∧ k ≤ n / 2 → P n k > c * k * Real.log k

entries.name: erdos_688.variants.lglglg_over_lglg_is_big_o
entries.doc: Erdős claims in [Er80] (p. 106) that it is not difficult to prove $\epsilon_n \gg \frac{\log\log\log n}{\log\log n}$.
entries.statement: (fun (n : ℕ) ↦ (log (log (log (n : ℝ)))) / (log (log (n : ℝ)))) =O[atTop] epsilonFunction

entries.name: erdos_730.variants.delta_ne_one
entries.doc: For example, $(87,88)$ and $(607,608)$ are such pairs. -/ @[category textbook, AMS 11] theorem erdos_730.variants.explicit_pairs : {(87, 88), (607, 608)} ⊆ S := by rintro _ (rfl | rfl) <;> exact ⟨by decide, by native_decide⟩ /-- There are examples where $(n, m) ∈ S$ with $m ≠ n + 1$. (Found by AlphaProof, although it was implicit already in [A129515])

entries.name: erdos_757.variants.lowerBound
entries.doc: The supremum is strictly larger than `1 / 2`, which is proved in [GyLe95].
entries.statement: Set ℝ} : 1 / (2 : ℝ) < sSup {c | IsAdmissible c}

entries.name: erdos_757.variants.upperBound
entries.doc: In [GyLe95], the authors also prove that the supremum is smaller than `3 / 5`.
entries.statement: Set ℝ} : sSup {c | IsAdmissible c} < 3 / (5 : ℝ)

entries.name: erdos_789.variants.isBigO_sq
entries.doc: Straus [Str66] proved that $h(n) \ll \sqrt{n}$.
entries.statement: (fun n ↦ (subsetSumThreshold n : ℝ)) =O[atTop] fun n ↦ √n

entries.name: erdos_789.variants.cube_root_linearithmic_isBigO
entries.doc: Erdős [Er62c] and Choi [Ch74b] proved that $(n\log(n))^{1/3}\ll h(n)$.
entries.statement: (fun n : ℕ ↦ (n * Real.log n) ^ ((1 : ℝ) / 3)) =O[atTop] fun n ↦ (subsetSumThreshold n : ℝ)

entries.name: erdos_817.variants.bdd_power
entries.doc: Define $g_k(n)$ to be the minimal $N$ such that $\{1, ..., N\}$ contains some $A$ of size $|A| = n$ such that $$ \langle A\rangle = \left\{\sum_{a \in A} \epsilon_a a : \epsilon_a \in\{0, 1\}\right\} $$ contains no non-trivial $k$-term arithmetic progression. -/ noncomputable def g (k : ℕ) (n : ℕ) : ℕ := sInf { N | ∃ A ⊆ Finset.Icc 1 N, A.card = n ∧ ∀ s, s ⊆ { ∑ a ∈ B, a | B ∈ A.powerset } → s.IsA
entries.statement: ∃ O > (0 : ℝ), (fun (n : ℕ) => (3 ^ n : ℝ) / n ^ O) =O[atTop] fun n => (g 3 n : ℝ)

entries.name: erdos_82.variants.F_upper_bound
entries.doc: $F(n) \le O(n^{1/2} \ln ^ {3/4} n)$ Theorem 1.4 from [AKS07] [AKS07] Alon, N. and Krivelevich, M. and Sudakov, B., Large nearly regular induced subgraphs. arXiv:0710.2106 (2007).
entries.statement: (fun n => (F n : ℝ)) =O[atTop] (fun n => Real.sqrt n * (Real.log n) ^ (3 / 4 : ℝ))

entries.name: mordell
entries.doc: There is exactly one ordered pair of cubes summing to $0$, namely $(0, 0)$. -/ @[category test, AMS 11] theorem sumRep_cubes_zero : sumRep cubes 0 = 1 := by sorry /-- The only ordered pair of cubes summing to $2$ is $(1, 1)$. -/ @[category test, AMS 11] theorem sumRep_cubes_two : sumRep cubes 2 = 1 := by sorry /-- The integer $3$ is not the sum of two cubes. -/ @[category test, AMS 11] theorem sum
entries.statement: limsup (fun n : ℕ => (sumRep cubes n : ℕ∞)) atTop = (⊤ : ℕ∞)

entries.name: mahler
entries.doc: Mahler proved $(1_A \ast 1_A)(n) \gg (\log n)^{1/4}$ for infinitely many $n$, where $A$ is the set of perfect cubes. [Ma35b] Mahler, K., _On the lattice points on curves of genus 1_. Proc. London Math. Soc. (2) (1935), 431-466.
entries.statement: ∃ C > (0 : ℝ), ∃ᶠ (n : ℕ) in atTop, C * (Real.log n) ^ ((1 : ℝ) / 4) ≤ (sumRep cubes n : ℝ)

entries.name: stewart
entries.doc: Stewart improved Mahler's lower bound to $(1_A \ast 1_A)(n) \gg (\log n)^{11/13}$ for infinitely many $n$, where $A$ is the set of perfect cubes. [St08] Stewart, C. L., _Cubic Thue equations with many solutions_. Int. Math. Res. Not. IMRN (2008), Art. ID rnn040, 11.
entries.statement: ∃ C > (0 : ℝ), ∃ᶠ (n : ℕ) in atTop, C * (Real.log n) ^ ((11 : ℝ) / 13) ≤ (sumRep cubes n : ℝ)

entries.name: erdos_830.variants.erdos
entries.doc: We say that $a,b\in \mathbb{N}$ are an amicable pair if $\sigma(a)=\sigma(b)=a+b$. If $A(x)$ counts the number of amicable $1\leq a\leq b\leq x$ then one can show that $A(x) = o(x)$.
entries.statement: A =o[atTop] id

entries.name: erdos_830.variants.pomerance
entries.doc: We say that $a,b\in \mathbb{N}$ are an amicable pair if $\sigma(a)=\sigma(b)=a+b$. If $A(x)$ counts the number of amicable $1\leq a\leq b\leq x$ then one can show that $A(x) \leq x \exp(-(\log x)^{1/3})$.
entries.statement: ∀ᶠ x in atTop, A x ≤ x * rexp (- Real.nthRoot 3 x.log)

entries.name: erdos_830.variants.pomerance_stronger
entries.doc: We say that $a,b\in \mathbb{N}$ are an amicable pair if $\sigma(a)=\sigma(b)=a+b$. If $A(x)$ counts the number of amicable $1\leq a\leq b\leq x$ then one can show that $A(x) \leq x \exp(-(\tfrac{1}{2}+o(1))(\log x\log\log x)^{1/2})$.
entries.statement: ∃ o : ℝ → ℝ, o =o[atTop] (1 : ℝ → ℝ) ∧ ∀ᶠ x in atTop, A x ≤ x * rexp (- (1/ 2 + o x) * √(x.log * x.log.log))

entries.name: erdos_847
entries.doc: `HasFew3APs A` means that $A \subset \mathbb{N}$ is a set for which there exists some $\epsilon > 0$ such that in any subset of $A$ of size $n$ there is a subset of size at least $\epsilon n$ which contains no three-term arithmetic progression. -/ def HasFew3APs (A : Set ℕ) := ∃ (ε : ℝ), ε > 0 ∧ ∀ (B : Set ℕ), B ⊆ A → Finite B → ∃ (C : Set ℕ), C ⊆ B ∧ C.ncard ≥ ε * B.ncard ∧ ThreeAPFree C /-- Let
entries.statement: answer(False) ↔ ∀ (A : Set ℕ), Infinite A → HasFew3APs A → ∃ n, ∃ (S : Fin n → Set ℕ), (∀ i, ThreeAPFree (S i)) ∧ A = ⋃ i : Fin n, S i

entries.name: erdos_848
entries.doc: A set $A$ has the non-squarefree product property if $ab + 1$ is not squarefree for all $a, b ∈ A$. -/ def NonSquarefreeProductProp (A : Finset ℕ) : Prop := ∀ a ∈ A, ∀ b ∈ A, ¬Squarefree (a * b + 1) /-- The candidate extremal set: $\{n ∈ \{0, \dots, N-1\} : n ≡ 7 (mod 25)\}$. -/ def A₇ (N : ℕ) : Finset ℕ := (Finset.range N).filter (fun n => n % 25 = 7) /-- The Erdős Problem 848 statement for a fix
entries.statement: answer(True) ↔ ∀ N, Erdos848For N

entries.name: erdos_851.variants.romanoff
entries.doc: `TwoPowAddSet r` is the set of integers of the form `2^k+n`, where `k ≥ 0` and `n` has at most `r` prime divisors. -/ def TwoPowAddSet (r : ℕ) := {(2 ^ k + n) | (k : ℕ) (n : ℕ) (_ : n.primeFactors.card ≤ r)} /-- The set of integers of the form `2^k+p` (where `p` is prime) has positive lower density. Formalisation note: here we also allow `p = 1` since this simplifies the code and is equivalent to
entries.statement: 0 < Set.lowerDensity (TwoPowAddSet 1)

entries.name: erdos_851
entries.doc: Let $\epsilon > 0$. Is there some $r \ll_\epsilon 1$ such that the density of integers of the form $2^k+n$, where $k \geq 0$ and $n$ has at most $r$ prime divisors, is at least $1-\epsilon$? This was proved affirmatively by Price and GPT-5.2 Pro [Pr26].
entries.statement: ℝ) (hε : ε ∈ Set.Ioo 0 1) : ∃ r d, (TwoPowAddSet r).HasDensity d ∧ 1 - ε ≤ d

entries.name: erdos_859.variants.erdos_upper_lower_bounds
entries.doc: `DivisorSumSet t` is the set of natural numbers `n` such that `t` can be represented as a sum of distinct divisors of `n`. -/ def DivisorSumSet (t : ℕ) := { n : ℕ | ∃ s ⊆ Nat.divisors n, t = ∑ i ∈ s, i } open Asymptotics Filter /-- A weaker version of the problem proved by Erdos: The density `dₜ` of `DivisorSumSet (t : ℕ)` is bounded from below by `1 / log (t) ^ c₃` and from above by `1 / log (t)
entries.statement: ∃ᵉ (c₃ > (0 : ℝ)) (c₄ > (0 : ℝ)) (t₀ : ℕ), ∀ᶠ t in atTop, ∃ dₜ : ℝ, (DivisorSumSet t).HasDensity dₜ ∧ 1 / Real.log t ^ c₃ < dₜ ∧ dₜ < 1 / Real.log t ^ c₄

entries.name: erdos_865.variants.upper_bound
entries.doc: Choi, Erdős, and Szemerédi [CES75] have proved that, for all $k\geq 3$, there exists $\epsilon_k>0$ such that (for large enough $N$) $f_k(N)\leq \left(\frac{2}{3}-\epsilon_k\right)N$.
entries.statement: ℕ) (hk : 3 ≤ k) : ∃ ε > 0, ∀ᶠ N in atTop, (f N k : ℝ) ≤ (2 / 3 - ε) * N

entries.name: erdos_884
entries.doc: The sum $\sum_{1 \le i < j \le \tau(n)} \frac{1}{d_j - d_i}$ over all pairs of divisors $d_i < d_j$ of $n$. -/ noncomputable abbrev sumDivisorInvPairwiseDifference (n : ℕ) : ℝ := ∑ j : Fin n.divisors.card, ∑ i : Fin j, (1 : ℚ) / (Nat.nth (· ∣ n) j - Nat.nth (· ∣ n) i ) /-- The sum $\sum_{1 \le i < \tau(n)} \frac{1}{d_{i + 1} - d_i}$ over consecutive divisors of $n$. -/ noncomputable abbrev sumDivi
entries.statement: answer(False) ↔ Erdos884Prop

entries.name: erdos_884_false_of_hardy_littlewood
entries.doc: In September 2025, Terence Tao gave a conditional _negative_ answer to Erdos conjecture 884, disproving it under the assumption of the *Qualitative Hardy-Littlewood Conjecture*. See [here](https://terrytao.wordpress.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/09/erdos-884.pdf). The *qualitative* version of the conjecture only states that there are infinitely many tuples of primes and does not require any asymptot
entries.statement: ∀ (k : ℕ) (m : Fin k.succ → ℕ), HardyLittlewood.FirstHardyLittlewoodConjectureFor m → ¬Erdos884Prop

entries.name: erdos_885.variants.k_eq_2
entries.doc: Erdős and Rosenfeld [ErRo97] proved this is true for $k=2$.
entries.statement: ∃ Ns : Finset ℕ, (∀ n ∈ Ns, 1 ≤ n) ∧ Ns.card = 2 ∧ (⋂ n ∈ Ns, factorDifferenceSet n).ncard ≥ 2

entries.name: erdos_885.variants.k_eq_3
entries.doc: Jiménez-Urroz [Ji99] proved this for $k=3$.
entries.statement: ∃ Ns : Finset ℕ, (∀ n ∈ Ns, 1 ≤ n) ∧ Ns.card = 3 ∧ (⋂ n ∈ Ns, factorDifferenceSet n).ncard ≥ 3

entries.name: erdos_885.variants.k_eq_4
entries.doc: Bremner [Br19] proved this for $k=4$.
entries.statement: ∃ Ns : Finset ℕ, (∀ n ∈ Ns, 1 ≤ n) ∧ Ns.card = 4 ∧ (⋂ n ∈ Ns, factorDifferenceSet n).ncard ≥ 4

entries.name: erdos_886.variants.rosenfeld_infinite
entries.doc: Erdős and Rosenfeld [ErRo97] proved that there are infinitely many $n$ such that there are four divisors of $n$ in $(n^{1/2},n^{1/2}+16n^{1/4})$.
entries.statement: Set.Infinite {n | 4 ≤ (Erdos886Divisors n (1/4) 16).card}

entries.name: erdos_888.variants.sarkozy
entries.statement: (fun n ↦ (Nat.findGreatest (p n) n : ℝ)) =o[atTop] (Nat.cast : ℕ → ℝ)

entries.name: erdos_888.variants.primes
entries.doc: The primes show that `|A| ≫ n/log n` is possible.
entries.statement: (fun n : ℕ ↦ (Nat.findGreatest (p n) n : ℝ )) ≫ (fun n : ℕ ↦ n / (n : ℝ).log)

entries.name: erdos_893.variants.unbounded
entries.doc: Kovač and Luca [KoLu25] (building on a heuristic independently found by Cambie (personal communication)) have shown that there is no finite limit, in that $\lim_{n\to\infty} \frac{f(2n)}{f(n)}$ is unbounded.
entries.statement: ¬ BddAbove (Set.range fun n : ℕ => (f (2 * n) : ℝ) / f n)

entries.name: erdos_9.variants.infinite
entries.doc: The set of odd numbers that cannot be expressed as a prime plus two powers of 2. -/ def Erdos9A : Set ℕ := { n | Odd n ∧ ¬ ∃ (p k l : ℕ), (Nat.Prime p) ∧ n = p + 2 ^ k + 2 ^ l } @[category test, AMS 5 11] theorem erdos9A_contains_one : 1 ∈ Erdos9A := by constructor · decide · push_neg intro p k l hp linarith [Nat.Prime.two_le hp, @Nat.one_le_two_pow k, @Nat.one_le_two_pow l] @[category test, AMS 5
entries.statement: Erdos9A.Infinite

entries.name: sawin_lattice_reduction
entries.doc: Sawin's explicit bound implies the qualitative polynomial lower bound, by taking $c = 0.014114$. -/ @[category test, AMS 52] theorem erdos_90.variants.sawin_explicit_implies_polynomial_lower_bound : type_of% erdos_90.variants.sawin_explicit → type_of% erdos_90.variants.polynomial_lower_bound := by intro h exact ⟨0.014114, by norm_num, by convert h; norm_num⟩ /-- The polynomial lower bound implies
entries.statement: ℕ) (hd : 1 ≤ d) (R : ℝ) (hR : 2 ≤ R) (Λ : Submodule ℤ (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin (2*d)))) (π : Λ →+ ℝ²) (hπ : Function.Injective π) (S : Finset Λ) (hS_norm : ∀ v ∈ S, ‖(v : EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin (2*d)))‖ ≤ 1) (hS_proj : ∀ v ∈ S, ‖π v‖ = 1) : ∃ U : Finset ℝ², 0 < U.card ∧ ((1 : ℝ) - 1/R) ^ (2*d) * (S.card : ℝ) * (U.card : ℝ) ≤ (unitDistancePairsCount U : ℝ)

entries.name: erdos_912.variants.selfridge
entries.doc: If $n! = \prod_{i}p_i^{k_i}$ is the factorization into distinct primes, then we define $h(n)$ to be the number of distinct exponents $k_i$. -/ noncomputable def h (n : ℕ) : ℕ := (n !).factorization.frange.card /-- Erdős and Selfridge prove in [Er82c] that $h(n) \asymp \left(\frac{n}{\log n}\right)^{1/2}$.
entries.statement: (fun n => (h n : ℝ)) =Θ[atTop] (fun n => (n / Real.log n) ^ (1 / 2 : ℝ))

entries.name: erdos_920.variants.upper_bound
entries.doc: Graver and Yackel [GrYa68] proved that $f_k(n) \ll \left(n\frac{\log\log n}{\log n}\right)^{1-\frac{1}{k-1}}.$
entries.statement: ℕ) (hk : k ≥ 3) : (fun n ↦ f k n) ≪ (fun n ↦ ((n : ℝ) * log (log n) / log n) ^ (1 - 1 / ((k : ℝ) - 1)))

entries.name: erdos_920.variants.k_eq_3
entries.doc: It is known that $f_3(n)\asymp (n/\log n)^{1/2}$ (see [erdosproblems.com/1104]).
entries.statement: (fun n ↦ (f 3 n : ℝ)) =Θ[atTop] (fun n ↦ ((n : ℝ) / log n) ^ (1 / 2 : ℝ))

entries.name: erdos_920.variants.lower_bound_f4
entries.doc: The lower bound $R(4,m) \gg m^3/(\log m)^4$ of Mattheus and Verstraete [MaVe23] (see [erdosproblems.com/166]) implies $f_4(n) \gg \frac{n^{2/3}}{(\log n)^{4/3}}$.

entries.name: erdos_920.variants.lower_bound
entries.doc: A positive answer to this question would follow from [erdosproblems.com/986]. The known bounds for that problem imply $f_k(n) \gg \frac{n^{1-\frac{2}{k+1}}}{(\log n)^{c_k}}.$

entries.name: erdos_930.variants.consecutive_strong
entries.doc: Returns the least prime satisfying $k \le p$ -/ def nextPrime (k : ℕ) : ℕ := Nat.find (Nat.exists_infinite_primes k) /-- Let $k$, $l$, $n$ be integers such that $k \ge 3$, $l \ge 2$ and $n + k \ge p^{(k)}$, where $p^{(k)}$ is the least prime satisfying $p^{(k)} \ge k$. Then there is a prime $p \ge k$ for which $l$ does not divide the multiplicity of the prime factor $p$ in $(n + 1) \ldots (n + k)$
entries.statement: ∀ k l n, 3 ≤ k → 2 ≤ l → nextPrime k ≤ n + k → ∃ p, k ≤ p ∧ p.Prime ∧ ¬ (l ∣ Nat.factorization (∏ m ∈ Icc (n + 1) (n + k), m) p)

entries.name: erdos_930.variants.consecutive_integers
entries.doc: Erdos and Selfridge [ErSe75] proved that the product of consecutive integers is never a power (establishing the case $r=1$). Theorem 1 from [ErSe75]. It is implied from `erdos_930.variants.consecutive_strong`. [ErSe75] Erdős, P. and Selfridge, J. L., The product of consecutive integers is never a power. Illinois J. Math. (1975), 292-301.
entries.statement: ∀ n k, 0 ≤ n → 2 ≤ k → ¬ IsPower (∏ m ∈ Icc (n + 1) (n + k), m)

entries.name: erdos_939.variants.examples
entries.doc: Cambie has found several examples of the sum of $r - 2$ coprime $r$-powerful numbers being itself $r$-powerful. For example when $r=5$ we have $$3761^5=2^8\cdot3^{10}\cdot 5^7 + 2^{12}\cdot 23^6 + 11^5\cdot 13^5$$.
entries.statement: (∃ r ≥ 4, (Erdos939Sums r).Nonempty)

entries.name: erdos_939.variants.seven
entries.doc: Cambie has also found solutions when $r=7$.
entries.statement: (Erdos939Sums 7).Nonempty

entries.name: erdos_939.variants.eight
entries.doc: Cambie has also found solutions when $r=8$.
entries.statement: (Erdos939Sums 8).Nonempty

entries.name: erdos_944.variants.dirac_conjecture.k_eq_5
entries.doc: Dirac's conjecture was proved, for $k=5$: There exists a graph $G$ with chromatic number $5$, such that every vertex is critical, yet every critical set of edges has size $>1$, or in other words: has no critical edge. [Br92] Brown, Jason I., A vertex critical graph without critical edges. Discrete Math. (1992), 99--101
entries.statement: ∃ (V : Type u) (G : SimpleGraph V), G.IsErdos944 5 1

entries.name: erdos_944.variants.dirac_conjecture.k_sub_one_not_prime
entries.doc: Lattanzio [La02] proved there exist $k$-critical graphs without critical edges for all $k$ such that $k - 1$ is not prime. [La02] Lattanzio, John J., A note on a conjecture of {D}irac. Discrete Math. (2002), 323--330
entries.statement: ℕ) (hk : 4 ≤ k) (h : ¬ (k - 1).Prime) : ∃ (V : Type u) (G : SimpleGraph V), G.IsErdos944 k 1

entries.name: erdos_944.variants.dirac_conjecture.k_ge_five
entries.doc: Jensen [Je02] gave an construction for $k$-critical graphs without any critical edges for all $k ≥ 5$. [Je02] Jensen, Tommy R., Dense critical and vertex-critical graphs. Discrete Math. (2002), 63--84.
entries.statement: ℕ) (hk : 5 ≤ k) : ∃ (V : Type u) (G : SimpleGraph V), G.IsErdos944 k 1

entries.name: erdos_944.variants.large_k_for_any_r
entries.doc: Martinsson and Steiner [MaSt25] proved for every $r \ge 1$ if $k$ is sufficiently large, depending on $r$, there exist a graph $G$ with chromatic number $k$ such that every vertex is critical, yet every critical set of edges has size $>r$. [MaSt25] Martinsson, Anders and Steiner, Raphael, Vertex-critical graphs far from edge-criticality. Combin. Probab. Comput. (2025), 151--157
entries.statement: ℕ) (hr : 1 ≤ r) : ∀ᶠ k in Filter.atTop, ∃ (V : Type u) (G : SimpleGraph V), G.IsErdos944 k r

entries.name: erdos_945.variants.lower_bound
entries.doc: The two ways of phrasing the conjecture are equivalent. -/ @[category textbook, AMS 11] theorem erdos_945.variants.equivalence : Erdos945Prop ↔ Erdos945Constant := by sorry /-- Erdős and Mirsky [ErMi52] proved that $\frac{(\log x)^{1/2}}{\log\log x}\ll F(x)$.
entries.statement: (fun (x : ℕ) => (log x).sqrt /(log x).log) =O[atTop] fun (n : ℕ) => (F n : ℝ)

entries.name: erdos_945.variants.upper_bound
entries.doc: Erdős and Mirsky [ErMi52] proved that $\log F(x) \ll \frac{(\log x)^{1/2}}$.
entries.statement: (fun (n : ℕ) => (F n : ℝ).log) =O[atTop] fun (x : ℕ) => (log x).sqrt /(log x).log

entries.name: erdos_946.variants.heathbrown_lower_bound
entries.doc: Number of $n \le x$ with $τ(n) = τ(n+1)$. -/ noncomputable def erdos946Count (x : ℝ) : ℝ := ((Finset.range (⌊x⌋₊ + 1)).filter (fun n => σ 0 n = σ 0 (n + 1))).card /-- The number of $n \le x$ with $τ(n) = τ(n+1)$ is at least $x / (\log x)^7$ for all sufficiently large $x$. Proved in [He84].
entries.statement: (fun x => x / (x.log)^7) =O[atTop] erdos946Count

entries.name: erdos_946.variants.hildebrand_lower_bound
entries.doc: Improved lower bound in [Hi85]: $Ω(x / (\log \log x)^3)$.
entries.statement: (fun x => x / (x.log.log)^3) =O[atTop] erdos946Count

entries.name: erdos_946.variants.upper_bound
entries.doc: Upper bound in [EPS87]: $O(x / \sqrt{\log \log x})$.
entries.statement: erdos946Count =O[atTop] (fun x => x / √x.log.log )

entries.name: erdos_961.sylvester_schur
entries.doc: Sylvester and Schur [Er34] proved that every set of $k$ consecutive integers greater than $k$ contains an integer divisible by a prime greater than $k$, i.e. not $(k+1)$-smooth.
entries.statement: ℕ) (hk : 0 < k) : Erdos961Prop k k

entries.name: erdos_961.variants.well_defined
entries.doc: There exists $n$ such that `Erdos961Prop k n` holds.
entries.statement: ℕ) (hk : 0 < k): ∃ n, Erdos961Prop k n

entries.name: erdos_961.variants.erdos_upper_bound
entries.doc: Erdos [Er55d] proved $f(k) < 3 \frac{k}{\log k}$ for sufficiently large $k$.
entries.statement: ∀ᶠ k in atTop, f k < 3 * k / log k

entries.name: erdos_961.variants.jutila_ramachandra_shorey_upper_bound
entries.doc: Jutila [Ju74], and Ramachandra--Shorey [RaSh73] proved a stronger upper bound $f(k) \ll \frac{\log \log \log k}{\log \log k} \frac{k}{\log k}$.
entries.statement: (fun k => (f k : ℝ)) =O[atTop] fun k => log (log (log k)) / log (log k) * (k / log k)

entries.name: erdos_962.variants.tang_lower_bound
entries.doc: Tang's lower bound [Tang]: $\log k(n) \ge (1/\sqrt{2} - o(1)) * \sqrt{\log n * \log \log n}$
entries.statement: ∃ ε : ℕ → ℝ, (∀ δ > 0, ∀ᶠ n in atTop, |ε n| < δ) ∧ ∀ᶠ n : ℕ in atTop, (1 / sqrt 2 - ε n) * sqrt (log n * log (log n)) ≤ log (k n : ℝ)

entries.name: erdos_962.variants.tao_upper_bound
entries.doc: Tao's upper bound [Tao]: $k(n) \le (1 + o(1)) * n^{1/2}$
entries.statement: ∃ ε : ℕ → ℝ, (∀ δ > 0, ∀ᶠ n in atTop, |ε n| < δ) ∧ ∀ᶠ n : ℕ in atTop, (k n : ℝ) ≤ (1 + ε n) * sqrt n

entries.name: erdos_968.variants.sum_abs_diff_isTheta_log_sq
entries.doc: Erdős and Prachar proved `∑_{pₙ < x} |u (n+1) - u n| ≍ (log x)^2` (see [ErPr61]). We encode `∑_{pₙ < x}` as a sum over `n < Nat.primeCounting' x` (the number of primes `< x`).
entries.statement: (fun x : ℕ => ∑ n < Nat.primeCounting' x, |u (n + 1) - u n|) =Θ[atTop] fun x : ℕ => log x ^ 2

entries.name: erdos_968.variants.decreasingSteps_hasPosDensity
entries.doc: Erdős and Prachar proved that the set `{n | u n > u (n+1)}` has positive natural density (see [ErPr61]).
entries.statement: {n : ℕ | u n > u (n + 1)}.HasPosDensity

entries.name: erdos_971.variants.infinite_sequence
entries.doc: Erdős [Er49c] proved that the statement in `erdos_971` holds for infinitely many values of `d`. [Er49c] Erdős, P., _On some applications of Brun's method_. Acta Univ. Szeged. Sect. Sci. Math. (1949), 57--63.
entries.statement: ∃ c > (0 : ℝ), ∃ C > (0 : ℝ), {d : ℕ | C * (d.totient : ℝ) ≤ #{a < d | a.Coprime d ∧ (leastCongruentPrime a d : ℝ) > (1 + c) * d.totient * log d}}.Infinite

entries.name: erdos_971.variants.many_small
entries.doc: Erdős [Er49c] proved that for any `ε > 0` we have `p(a, d) < ε * φ(d) * log d` for `≫_ε φ(d)` many values of `a` (for all large `d`). [Er49c] Erdős, P., _On some applications of Brun's method_. Acta Univ. Szeged. Sect. Sci. Math. (1949), 57--63.
entries.statement: ∀ ε > (0 : ℝ), ∃ C > (0 : ℝ), ∀ᶠ d in atTop, C * (d.totient : ℝ) ≤ #{a < d | a.Coprime d ∧ (leastCongruentPrime a d : ℝ) < ε * d.totient * log d}

entries.name: erdos_975.variants.upper_bound
entries.doc: The correctness of the growth rate is shown in [Va39] (lower bound) and [Er52b] (upper bound).
entries.statement: ℤ[X]) (hf : Irreducible f) (hf_pos : ∀ᶠ n in atTop, 1 ≤ f.eval n) : Erdos975Sum f =O[atTop] (fun x ↦ x * log x)

entries.name: erdos_975.variants.lower_bound
entries.doc: Lower bound for the growth rate of `Erdos975Sum`, shown in [Va39].
entries.statement: ℤ[X]) (hf : Irreducible f) (hfdeg : f.natDegree ≠ 0) (hf_pos : ∀ᶠ n in atTop, 1 ≤ f.eval n) : (fun x ↦ x * log x) =O[atTop] Erdos975Sum f

entries.name: erdos_975.variants.n2_plus_1_strong
entries.doc: More concrete example for $f(n) = n^2 + 1$, where the asymptote is $\sum_{n \le x} \tau(n^2 + 1) \sim \frac{3}{\pi} x \log x + O(x)$. See Tao's blog [T].
entries.statement: (fun x ↦ Erdos975Sum (X ^ 2 + 1) x - (3 / π) * x * log x) =O[atTop] id

entries.name: erdos_975.variants.n2_plus_1
entries.doc: Asymptotics for `Erdos975Sum` with $f(X) = X^2 + 1$.
entries.statement: ∃ c > (0 : ℝ), Tendsto (fun x ↦ Erdos975Sum (X ^ 2 + 1) x / (x * log x)) atTop (𝓝 c)

entries.name: erdos_979.variants.k2
entries.doc: Erdős [Er37b] proved that if $f_2(n)$ counts the number of solutions to $n = p_1^2 + p_2^2$, where $p_1$ and $p_2$ are prime numbers, then $\limsup f_2(n) = \infty$. [Er37b] Erdős, Paul, On the Sum and Difference of Squares of Primes. J. London Math. Soc. (1937), 133--136.
entries.statement: Filter.limsup (fun n => (solutionSet n 2).encard) Filter.atTop = ⊤

entries.name: erdos_979.variants.k3
entries.doc: Erdős (unpublished)
entries.statement: Filter.limsup (fun n => (solutionSet n 3).encard) Filter.atTop = ⊤

entries.name: erdos_997.variants.lacunary
entries.doc: Call $x_1,x_2,\ldots \in (0,1)$ well-distributed if, for every $\epsilon>0$, if $k$ is sufficiently large then, for all $n>0$ and intervals $I\subseteq [0,1]$, $\lvert \# \{ n < m\leq n+k : x_m\in I\} - \lvert I\rvert k\rvert < \epsilon k.$ The notion of a well-distributed sequence was introduced by Hlawka and Petersen [Hl55]. -/ def IsWellDistributed (x : ℕ → ℝ) : Prop := ∀ ε > 0, ∀ᶠ k in Filter.
entries.statement: ℕ → ℕ) (h : IsLacunary n) : ∀ᵐ α, ¬ IsWellDistributed (fun k ↦ Int.fract (α * (n k : ℝ)))

entries.name: erdos_997.variants.irrational
entries.doc: He also claimed in [Er64b] to have proved that there exists an irrational $\alpha$ for which $\{\alpha p_n\}$ is not well-distributed. He later retracted this claim in [Er85e], saying "The theorem is no doubt correct and perhaps will not be difficult to prove but I never was able to reconstruct my 'proof' which perhaps never existed." The existence of such an $\alpha$ was established by Champagne,
entries.statement: ∃ α : ℝ, Irrational α ∧ ¬ IsWellDistributed (fun n ↦ Int.fract (α * (n.nth Nat.Prime)))

entries.name: green_14_polynomial_k_eq_3
entries.doc: We know $W(3, r)$ does not have polynomial growth in $r$ [Gr21, p.3].
entries.statement: ¬ ∃ d : ℕ, (fun r => (W 3 r : ℝ)) =O[atTop] fun r => (r : ℝ) ^ d

entries.name: green_14_quadratic
entries.doc: Is $W(3, r) \ll r^2$? [Gr21] proves a superpolynomial lower bound $W(3, r) \gg \exp(c(\log r)^{4/3-o(1)})$.
entries.statement: answer(False) ↔ (fun r => (W 3 r : ℝ)) =O[atTop] fun r => (r : ℝ) ^ 2

entries.name: green_2_lower_bound_sanders
entries.doc: From [Sa21] it is known that there is always such an S with $|S| \gt (\log |A|)^{1+c}$.
entries.statement: ∃ c > (0 : ℝ), ∀ᶠ n : ℕ in atTop, ∀ A : Finset ℤ, A.card = n → maxRestrictedSumAvoidingSubsetSize A ≥ Real.log n ^ (1 + c)

entries.name: green_2_upper_bound_erdos
entries.doc: From [Er65] it is known that $M(A) \le \frac{1}{3}|A| + O(1)$.
entries.statement: ∃ C : ℝ, ∀ᶠ n in atTop, ∃ A : Finset ℤ, A.card = n ∧ (maxRestrictedSumAvoidingSubsetSize A : ℝ) ≤ A.card / 3 + C

entries.name: green_2_upper_bound_choi
entries.doc: From [Ch71] it is known that $M(A) \le |A|^{2/5 + o(1)}$.
entries.statement: ∃ (o : ℕ → ℝ) (_ : Tendsto o atTop (𝓝 0)), ∀ᶠ n in atTop, ∃ A : Finset ℤ, A.card = n ∧ (maxRestrictedSumAvoidingSubsetSize A : ℝ) ≤ A.card ^ (2 / 5 + o A.card)

entries.statement: ∃ C > (0 : ℝ), ∀ᶠ n in atTop, ∃ A : Finset ℤ, A.card = n ∧ (maxRestrictedSumAvoidingSubsetSize A : ℝ) < Real.exp (C * Real.sqrt (Real.log A.card))

entries.name: green_22.variants.green_sawhney_bound

entries.name: green_25.variants.lower_ess89
entries.doc: For $k \ll \log \log N$, this is true [Gr24]. NOTE: [ESS89] derive a precise constant for which this is true, but $\ll$ would imply that it works for any constant. We thus prefer a little-o statement.

entries.name: green_25.variants.upper_ess89

entries.name: green_25.variants.upper_ess89_trivial

entries.name: green_26
entries.doc: The vector space $\mathbb{F}_p^n$. -/ abbrev 𝔽 (p n : ℕ) [Fact p.Prime] := Fin n → ZMod p /-- The vector space $\mathbb{F}_3^n$. -/ abbrev 𝔽₃ (n : ℕ) := 𝔽 3 n /-- The standard cube in $\mathbb{F}_p^n$ is the set of points with coordinates in $\{0, 1\}$. -/ def StandardCube {p : ℕ} [Fact p.Prime] (n : ℕ) : Set (𝔽 p n) := {x | ∀ i, x i = 0 ∨ x i = 1} /-- A cube is the image of $\lbrace 0, 1\rbrace^n
entries.statement: ∀ n : ℕ, ∀ A : Fin 100 → Set (𝔽₃ n), (∀ i, IsCube (A i)) → ∑ i, A i = univ

entries.name: green_26.variants.yu25
entries.doc: [Yu25] has solved the original problem (with 100 replaced by 4)
entries.statement: ∀ n : ℕ, ∀ A : Fin 4 → Set (𝔽₃ n), (∀ i, IsCube (A i)) → ∑ i, A i = univ

entries.name: green_26.variants.alm91
entries.doc: [ALM91] showed that if 100 is replaced by $\leq c(p) \log n$ then the result is true for $\mathbb{F}^n_p$.
entries.statement: ∀ (p : ℕ) [Fact p.Prime], ∃ (k : ℕ → ℕ), ((fun n ↦ (k n : ℝ)) =O[atTop] fun n ↦ Real.log n) ∧ ∀ᶠ n in atTop, ∀ A : Fin (k n) → Set (𝔽 p n), (∀ i, IsCube (A i)) → ∑ i, A i = univ

entries.name: green_27.variants.lower_be23

entries.name: green_31.variants.upper_li69

entries.name: green_31.variants.upper_bfr23

entries.name: green_32.variants.sh20_general

entries.name: green_32.variants.sh20_sqrt
entries.doc: [Sh20] has used the polynomial method to show that this is true with 100 replaced by 2 [Gr24]. Note: More precisely [Sh20, Theorem 1] implies a gap of at least $\lfloor 2p/|A| - 2 \rfloor$. For a set $A$ of size $\lfloor \sqrt{p} \rfloor$, this guarantees a gap of at least $\lfloor 2\sqrt{p} \rfloor - 2$.
entries.statement: ∀ᶠ p in atTop, p.Prime → ∀ A : Finset (ZMod p), A.card = ⌊Real.sqrt p⌋₊ → ∃ c : (ZMod p)ˣ, HasGap (c • A) (⌊2 * Real.sqrt p⌋₊ - 2)

entries.name: green_32.variants.szemeredi_regime
entries.doc: In the regime $\omega(p) \sim c p$, this is Szemerédi's theorem [Gr24].
entries.statement: ∀ c, 0 < c ∧ c < 1 → ∀ ω : ℕ → ℝ, ω ~[atTop] (fun p ↦ c * p) → HasLargeGapDilate ω

entries.name: green_32.variants.dirichlet_regime
entries.doc: In the regime $\omega(p) \le c \log p$, this is basically Dirichlet's lower bound for the size of Bohr sets [Gr24].
entries.statement: ∃ c > 0, ∀ ω : ℕ → ℝ, (∀ᶠ p in atTop, 100 < ω p ∧ ω p ≤ c * Real.log p) → HasLargeGapDilate ω

entries.name: green_32.variants.finite_field
entries.doc: A set $A$ has a coset hole of size $L$ if there exists a subspace $W$ and a vector $v$ such that the affine space $v + W$ has size at least $L$ and is disjoint from $A$. -/ def HasCosetHole {n : ℕ} (A : Finset (Fin n → ZMod 2)) (L : ℕ) : Prop := ∃ W : Submodule (ZMod 2) (Fin n → ZMod 2), ∃ v : Fin n → ZMod 2, L ≤ Nat.card W ∧ ∀ w : W, v + (w : Fin n → ZMod 2) ∉ A /-- The empty set in $\mathbb{F}_2
entries.statement: ∀ᶠ n in atTop, ∀ A : Finset (Fin n → ZMod 2), A.card = ⌊Real.sqrt (2^n : ℝ)⌋₊ → HasCosetHole A ⌊100 * Real.sqrt (2^n : ℝ)⌋₊

entries.name: green_38.variants.best_lower

entries.name: green_38.variants.best_upper

entries.statement: ∀ ε > 0, ∀ᶠ n in atTop, LargestAdmissibleCardinality n ≤ (C₂ + ε) ^ n

entries.name: isWeakGiuga_iff_sum_primeFactors
entries.doc: A (weak) Giuga number is a composite number $n$ such that $$\sum_{i=1}^{n - 1}i^{\varphi(n)} \equiv -1\pmod{n}$$. -/ def IsWeakGiuga (n : ℕ) : Prop := n.Composite ∧ n ∣ 1 + ∑ i ∈ Finset.Ioo 0 n, i ^ φ n -- Formalisation note: refers to a Giuga number in the sense of -- https://www.cambridge.org/core/services/aop-cambridge-core/content/view/8A6841B3FDA442A8FAEC89AA702C16F6/S0008439500007244a.pdf/no

entries.name: isStrongGiuga_iff
entries.doc: A Carmichael number is a composite number `n` such that for all `b ≥ 1`, we have `b^n ≡ b (mod n)`. -/ def IsCarmichael (n : ℕ) : Prop := 1 < n ∧ ¬ n.Prime ∧ ∀ b ≥ 1, n.Coprime b → n.FermatPsp b /-- A composite Carmichael number is squarefree. -/ @[category textbook, AMS 11] theorem squarefree_of_isCarmichael {a : ℕ} (ha₁ : a.Composite) (ha₂ : IsCarmichael a) : Squarefree a := by have ha₂_forall :
entries.statement: ℕ} (ha : a.Composite) : IsStrongGiuga a ↔ IsCarmichael a ∧ ∃ n : ℕ, ∑ p ∈ a.primeFactors, (1 / p : ℚ) - 1 / a = n

entries.name: shitov_upper_bound
entries.doc: **Shitov's bound (2019)**: Every synchronizing DFA with $n$ states admits a synchronizing word of length at most $\left(\frac{7}{48} + \frac{2 \cdot 15625}{1597536}\right) n^3 + o(n^3)$, where the $o(n^3)$ term is uniform over all alphabets. This is the best known upper bound towards the Černý conjecture.
entries.statement: ∃ f : ℝ → ℝ, f =o[atTop] (fun n : ℝ => n ^ 3) ∧ ∀ {α : Type*} {σ : Type*} [Fintype σ] (M : DFA α σ) (hM : M.IsSynchronizing) , ∃ w : List α, M.IsSynchronizingWord w ∧ (w.length : ℝ) ≤ (7 / 48 + 2 * 15625 / 1597536) * (Fintype.card σ : ℝ) ^ 3 + f (Fintype.card σ : ℝ)

entries.doc: $M(n)$ is the number of monotone Boolean functions on $n$ variables. -/ def M (n : ℕ) : ℕ := Fintype.card {f : (Fin n → Bool) → Bool // Monotone f} /-- A Sperner family (antichain) of subsets of `Fin n`: a family of sets such that no member is a subset of another. -/ def IsSperner {n : ℕ} (A : Finset (Finset (Fin n))) : Prop := IsAntichain (fun s t => s ⊆ t) (A : Set (Finset (Fin n))) instance isS
entries.statement: M = kisielewiczFormula

entries.name: union_closed.variants.yu
entries.doc: Yu [Yu23] showed that the union-closed sets conjecture holds with a constant of approximately 0.38234 instead of 1/2. [Yu23] Yu, Lei (2023). "Dimension-free bounds for the union-closed sets conjecture". Entropy. 25 (5): 767.
entries.statement: A ≠ {∅}) (h_union_closed : IsUnionClosed A) : ∃ i : n, (0.38234 : ℚ) * #A ≤ #{x ∈ A | i ∈ x}

entries.name: union_closed.variants.univ_card
entries.doc: Vuckovic and Zivkovic [Vu17] showed that the union-closed sets conjecture holds for set families whose universal set has cardinality at most 12. [Vu17] Vuckovic, Bojan; Zivkovic, Miodrag (2017). "The 12-Element Case of Frankl's Conjecture" (PDF). IPSI BGD Transactions on Internet Research. 13 (1): 65.
entries.statement: A ≠ {∅}) (h_union_closed : IsUnionClosed A) (h_card : Fintype.card n ≤ 12) : ∃ i : n, (1 / 2 : ℚ) * #A ≤ #{x ∈ A | i ∈ x}

entries.name: union_closed.variants.family_card
entries.doc: Roberts and Simpson [Ro10] showed that the union-closed sets conjecture holds for set families of size at most 46. Their method, however, combined with the result of [Vu17], further shows that it holds for `#A ≤ 50` as well. [Ro10] Roberts, Ian; Simpson, Jamie (2010). "A note on the union-closed sets conjecture" (PDF). Australas. J. Combin. 47: 265–267. [Vu17] Vuckovic, Bojan; Zivkovic, Miodrag (2
entries.statement: A ≠ {∅}) (h_union_closed : IsUnionClosed A) (hA : #A ≤ 50) : ∃ i : n, (1 / 2 : ℚ) * #A ≤ #{x ∈ A | i ∈ x}

entries.name: union_closed.variants.univ_card_two
entries.doc: We can show the union-closed sets conjecture is true for the case where the universal set has cardinality 2, by brute force.
entries.statement: Finset (Finset (Fin 2))) (h_ne_singleton_empty : A ≠ {∅}) (h_union_closed : IsUnionClosed A) : ∃ i, (1 / 2 : ℚ) * #A ≤ #{x ∈ A | i ∈ x}

entries.name: union_closed.variants.singleton_mem
entries.doc: We can show the union-closed sets conjecture is true for the case where the set family contains some singleton.
entries.statement: IsUnionClosed A) (i : n) (hi : {i} ∈ A) : ∃ i, (1 / 2 : ℚ) * #A ≤ #{x ∈ A | i ∈ x}

entries.name: union_closed.variants.sharpness
entries.doc: The union-closed sets conjecture is sharp in the sense that if we replace the constant `1/2` with any larger constant, then the conjecture fails.
entries.statement: ℝ) (hc : 1 / 2 < c) : ¬ (∀ A : Finset (Finset n), A ≠ {∅} → IsUnionClosed A → ∃ i : n, c * #A ≤ #{x ∈ A | i ∈ x})

A vertex $v$ is in the $\alpha$-core if $\alpha(G - v) < \alpha(G)$. -/ noncompu

(n-j)!}{n! (n-k)!} a_i b_j $$ for $k = 0, 1, \dots, n$. -/ noncomputable def finiteAdditiveConvolution (n : ℕ) (p q : F[X]) : F[X] := let c := fun k => ∑ ij ∈ antidiagonal (k : ℕ), ((n - ij.1)! * (n - ij.2)! :

entries.name: four_3
entries.doc: Is it true that if $p(x)$ and $q(x)$ are monic real-rooted polynomials of degree $3$, then $$\frac{1}{\Phi_3(p\boxplus_n q)} \ge \frac{1}{\Phi_3(p)}+\frac{1}{\Phi_3(q)}?$$
entries.statement: answer(True ) ↔ ∀ (p q : ℝ[X]), FourProp p q 3
