entries.name: erdos_1128
entries.doc: **Erdős Problem 1128** (disproved by Prikry–Mills, 1978): Erdős asked whether every 2-colouring of $A \times B \times C$, where $|A| = |B| = |C| = \aleph_1$, must contain a monochromatic countable box $A_1 \times B_1 \times C_1$ with $|A_1| = |B_1| = |C_1| = \aleph_0$. The answer is **No**: Prikry and Mills constructed a 2-colouring of $\omega_1^3$ with no monochromatic countable box. Note: The po
entries.statement: answer(False) ↔ ∀ (A B C : Type) (_ : #A = aleph 1) (_ : #B = aleph 1) (_ : #C = aleph 1) (f : A → B → C → Fin 2), ∃ (A₁ : Set A) (B₁ : Set B) (C₁ : Set C), #A₁ = aleph 0 ∧ #B₁ = aleph 0 ∧ #C₁ = aleph 0 ∧ IsMonochromaticBox f A₁ B₁ C₁

entries.name: erdos_1128.variants.prikryMills_explicit
entries.doc: **Explicit form of Prikry–Mills**: There exists a 2-colouring of $\omega_1 \times \omega_1 \times \omega_1$ such that for every countably infinite $A_1, B_1, C_1 \subseteq \omega_1$, the box $A_1 \times B_1 \times C_1$ is not monochromatic. This is the content of the Prikry–Mills theorem (1978, unpublished), stated using Lean's ordinal type `{o : Ordinal // o < ω_ 1}` as the representation of $\om
entries.statement: ∃ (f : {o : Ordinal // o < ω_ 1} → {o : Ordinal // o < ω_ 1} → {o : Ordinal // o < ω_ 1} → Fin 2), ∀ (A₁ B₁ C₁ : Set {o : Ordinal // o < ω_ 1}), #A₁ = aleph 0 → #B₁ = aleph 0 → #C₁ = aleph 0 → ¬ IsMonochromaticBox f A₁ B₁ C₁

entries.name: erdos_119.parts.i
entries.doc: Let $z_i$ be an infinite sequence of complex numbers such that $|z_i| = 1$ for all $i \geq 1$. For $n \geq 1$ let $p_n(z) = \prod_{i \leq n} (z - z_i)$. -/ noncomputable def p (z : ℕ → ℂ) (n : ℕ) : ℂ → ℂ := fun w => ∏ i ∈ range n, (w - z i) /-- Let $M_n = \max_{|z| = 1} |p_n(z)|$. -/ noncomputable def M (z : ℕ → ℂ) (n : ℕ) : ℝ := sSup { (‖p z n w‖) | (w : ℂ) (_ : ‖w‖ = 1) } /-- Question 1: Is it t
entries.statement: answer(True) ↔ ∀ (z : ℕ → ℂ) (hz : ∀ i : ℕ, ‖z i‖ = 1), atTop.limsup (fun n => (M z n : EReal)) = ⊤

entries.name: erdos_119.parts.ii
entries.doc: Question 2: Is it true that there exists $c > 0$ such that for infinitely many $n$ we have $M_n > n^c$? Beck [Be91] proved that there exists some $c > 0$ such that $\max_{n \leq N} M_n > N^c$. [Be91] Beck, J., The modulus of polynomials with zeros on the unit circle: A problem of Erdős. Annals of Math. (1991), 609-651.
entries.statement: answer(True) ↔ ∀ (z : ℕ → ℂ) (hz : ∀ i : ℕ, ‖z i‖ = 1), ∃ (c : ℝ) (hc : c > 0), Infinite {n : ℕ | M z n > n ^ c}

entries.name: erdos_184.variants.n_log_n
entries.doc: Erdős and Gallai [EGP66] proved that $O(n \log n)$ many cycles and edges suffices.
entries.statement: ∃ f : ℕ → ℝ, (f =O[atTop] fun n : ℕ ↦ (n : ℝ) * Real.log (n : ℝ)) ∧ ∀ {V : Type*} [Fintype V] [DecidableEq V] (G : SimpleGraph V), ∃ (D : Finset G.Subgraph), (∀ H ∈ D, IsCycleOrEdge H.coe) ∧ IsDecomposition G D ∧ (D.card : ℝ) ≤ f (Fintype.card V)

entries.name: erdos_184.variants.lower_bound
entries.doc: The graph $K_{3,n-3}$ shows that at least $(1+c)n$ many cycles and edges are required, for some constant $c>0$.
entries.statement: ∃ c > 0, ∀ᶠ n in atTop, let G : SimpleGraph (Fin n) := fromRel (fun (i j : Fin n) => (i : ℕ) < 3 ∧ 3 ≤ (j : ℕ)); ∀ (D : Finset G.Subgraph), (∀ H ∈ D, IsCycleOrEdge H.coe) → IsDecomposition G D → (1 + c) * (n : ℝ) ≤ (D.card : ℝ)

entries.name: erdos_184.variants.bucic_montgomery
entries.doc: The best bound available is due to Bucić and Montgomery [BM22], who prove that $O(n\log^* n)$ many cycles and edges suffice, where $\log^*$ is the iterated logarithm function.
entries.statement: ∃ f : ℕ → ℝ, (f =O[atTop] fun n : ℕ ↦ (n : ℝ) * (Real.iteratedLog (n : ℝ) : ℝ)) ∧ ∀ {V : Type*} [Fintype V] [DecidableEq V] (G : SimpleGraph V), ∃ (D : Finset G.Subgraph), (∀ H ∈ D, IsCycleOrEdge H.coe) ∧ IsDecomposition G D ∧ (D.card : ℝ) ≤ f (Fintype.card V)

entries.name: erdos_184.variants.conlon_fox_sudakov
entries.doc: Conlon, Fox, and Sudakov [CFS14] proved that $O_\epsilon(n)$ cycles and edges suffice if $G$ has minimum degree at least $\epsilon n$, for any $\epsilon>0$.
entries.statement: ∀ ε > 0, ∃ f : ℕ → ℝ, (f =O[atTop] fun n : ℕ ↦ (n : ℝ)) ∧ ∀ {V : Type*} [Fintype V] [DecidableEq V] (G : SimpleGraph V), (G.minDegree : ℝ) ≥ ε * (Fintype.card V : ℝ) → ∃ (D : Finset G.Subgraph), (∀ H ∈ D, IsCycleOrEdge H.coe) ∧ IsDecomposition G D ∧ (D.card : ℝ) ≤ f (Fintype.card V)

entries.name: erdos_189.variants.square
entries.doc: Erdős problem 189 asked whether the below holds for all rectangles. -/ def Erdos189For (P : ℝ² → ℝ² → ℝ² → ℝ² → Prop) (A : ℝ² → ℝ² → ℝ² → ℝ² → ℝ) := ∀ᵉ (n > 0) (colouring : ℝ² → Fin n), ∃ colour, ∀ area > (0 : ℝ), ∃ a b c d, {a, b, c, d} ⊆ colouring⁻¹' {colour} ∧ IsCcwConvexPolygon ![a, b, c, d] ∧ A a b c d = area ∧ P a b c d /-- If $\mathbb{R}^2$ is finitely coloured then must there exist some co
entries.statement: ¬ Erdos189For (fun a b c d ↦ line[ℝ, a, b].direction ⟂ line[ℝ, b, c].direction ∧ line[ℝ, b, c].direction ⟂ line[ℝ, c, d].direction ∧ line[ℝ, c, d].direction ⟂ line[ℝ, d, a].direction ∧ dist a b = dist b c) (fun a b c d ↦ dist a b * dist b c)

entries.name: erdos_213.variants.KK08
entries.doc: The best construction to date, due to Kreisel and Kurz, has $n = 7$.
entries.statement: Erdos213For 7

entries.name: erdos_254.variants.cassels
entries.doc: Cassels [Ca60] proved this under the alternative hypotheses $\lim \frac{\lvert A\cap [1,2x]\rvert -\lvert A\cap [1,x]\rvert}{\log\log x}=\infty$ and $\sum_{n\in A} \{ \theta n\}^2=\infty$ for every $\theta\in (0,1)$.
entries.statement: ∀ (A : Set ℕ), (Tendsto (fun x : ℕ ↦ (((A ∩ Icc 1 (2 * x)).ncard : ℝ) - ((A ∩ Icc 1 x).ncard : ℝ)) / Real.log (Real.log x)) atTop atTop) ∧ (∀ θ : ℝ, 0 < θ → θ < 1 → ¬ Summable (fun n : A ↦ (distToNearestInt (θ * (n : ℝ)))^2)) → ∀ᶠ m in atTop, IsSumOfDistinct A m

entries.name: erdos_274.variants.abelian
entries.doc: If `G` is a finite abelian group then there cannot exist an exact covering of `G` by more than one cosets of different sizes? (i.e. each element is contained in exactly one of the cosets.)
entries.statement: Type*} [Fintype G] [CommGroup G] (hG : 1 < Fintype.card G) {ι : Type*} [Fintype ι] (P : Group.ExactCovering G ι) (hι : 1 < Fintype.card ι) : ∃ i j, i ≠ j ∧ #(P.parts i) = #(P.parts j)

entries.name: exists_t_for_k_disjoint_segments
entries.doc: For any $k$ there exists some $t_k\in (0,1)$ such that the set of $\alpha$ such that the sequence $\lfloor t_k\alpha^n\rfloor$ is complete consists of at least $k$ disjoint line segments.
entries.statement: ℕ) : ∃ t ∈ Ioo 0 1, ∃ (ι : Type), k ≤ (Set.univ : Set ι).encard ∧ ∃ I : ι → Set ℝ, (∀ i, 2 ≤ (I i).encard ∧ (I i).Nonempty ∧ IsConnected (I i)) ∧ Pairwise (Disjoint on I) ∧ (⋃ i, I i) ⊆ {α | α > 0 ∧ IsGoodPair t α}

entries.name: erdos_43.parts.i
entries.doc: Let $f(N)$ be the maximum possible size of a Sidon set in $\{1,\ldots,N\}$. -/ noncomputable abbrev f (N : ℕ) : ℕ := Finset.maxSidonSubsetCard (Finset.Icc 1 N) /-- If $A$ and $B$ are Sidon sets in $\{1,\ldots,N\}$ with $(A-A)\cap(B-B)=\{0\}$, is it true that $$\binom{\lvert A\rvert}{2}+\binom{\lvert B\rvert}{2}\leq\binom{f(N)}{2}+O(1)?$$ The answer is no; the Erdős Problems page notes that this fo
entries.statement: answer(False) ↔ ∃ C : ℝ, ∀ᶠ N in Filter.atTop, ∀ (A B : Finset ℕ), A ⊆ Finset.Icc 1 N → B ⊆ Finset.Icc 1 N → IsSidon (A : Set ℕ) → IsSidon (B : Set ℕ) → (A - A) ∩ (B - B) = {0} → ((A.card.choose 2 + B.card.choose 2 : ℕ) : ℝ) ≤ ((f N).choose 2 : ℝ) + C

entries.name: erdos_43.parts.ii
entries.doc: If $A$ and $B$ are equal-sized Sidon sets in $\{1,\ldots,N\}$ with $(A-A)\cap(B-B)=\{0\}$, can the bound be improved to $$\binom{\lvert A\rvert}{2}+\binom{\lvert B\rvert}{2} \leq (1-c+o(1))\binom{f(N)}{2}$$ for some constant $c>0$? The answer is no; the Erdős Problems page records a negative answer due to Barreto.
entries.statement: answer(False) ↔ ∃ᵉ (c > 0), ∃ o : ℕ → ℝ, o =o[Filter.atTop] (1 : ℕ → ℝ) ∧ ∀ᶠ N in Filter.atTop, ∀ (A B : Finset ℕ), A ⊆ Finset.Icc 1 N → B ⊆ Finset.Icc 1 N → IsSidon (A : Set ℕ) → IsSidon (B : Set ℕ) → A.card = B.card → (A - A) ∩ (B - B) = {0} → ((A.card.choose 2 + B.card.choose 2 : ℕ) : ℝ) ≤ (1 - c + o N) * ((f N).choose 2 : ℝ)

entries.name: erdos_442
entries.doc: The function $\operatorname{Log} x := \max\{log x, 1\}$. -/ noncomputable def Real.maxLogOne (x : ℝ) := max x.log 1 namespace Set variable (A : Set ℕ) (x : ℝ) /-- If `A` be a set of natural numbers and let `x` be real, then `A.bddProdUpper x` is the finite upper-triangular set of pairs of elements of `A` that are `≤ x`. Specifically, it is the set `{(n, m) | n ∈ A, n ≤ x, m ∈ A, m ≤ x, n < m}` -/
entries.statement: answer(False) ↔ ∀ (A : Set ℕ), Tendsto (fun (x : ℝ) => 1 / x.maxLogOne.maxLogOne * ∑ n ∈ (A ∩ Icc 1 ⌊x⌋₊ : Set ℕ), (1 : ℝ) / n) atTop atTop → Tendsto (fun (x : ℝ) => 1 / (∑ n ∈ (A ∩ Icc 1 ⌊x⌋₊ : Set ℕ), (1 : ℝ) / n) ^ 2 * ∑ nm ∈ A.bddProdUpper x, (1 : ℝ) / nm.1.lcm nm.2) atTop atTop

entries.name: erdos_442.variants.tao
entries.doc: Tao resolved erdos_442 in the negative in Theorem 1 of https://arxiv.org/pdf/2407.04226. The following is a formalisation of that theorem with $C_0 = 1$. Let $\operatorname{Log} x := \max\{\log x, 1\}$, $\operatorname{Log}_2x = \operatorname{Log} (\operatorname{Log} x)$, and $\operatorname{Log}_3x = \operatorname{Log}(\operatorname{Log}(\operatorname{Log} x)).$ There exists a set $A$ of natural nu
entries.statement: ∃ (A : Set ℕ) (f : ℝ → ℝ) (C: ℝ) (hC : 0 < C) (hf : f =o[atTop] (1 : ℝ → ℝ)), ∀ᶠ (x : ℝ) in atTop, ∑ n ∈ (A ∩ Icc 1 ⌊x⌋₊ : Set ℕ), (1 : ℝ) / n = Real.exp ((1 / 2 + f x) * √x.maxLogOne.maxLogOne * x.maxLogOne.maxLogOne.maxLogOne) ∧ |∑ nm ∈ ((A ∩ Icc 1 ⌊x⌋₊) ×ˢ (A ∩ Icc 1 ⌊x⌋₊)).toFinset, (1 : ℝ) / nm.1.lcm nm.2| ≤ C * (∑ n ∈ (A ∩ Icc 1 ⌊x⌋₊ : Set ℕ), (1 : ℝ) / n) ^ 2

entries.name: erdos_507.variants.lower_kps82
entries.doc: Current best lower bound [KPS82].
entries.statement: lowerBest ≪ α

entries.name: erdos_507.variants.upper_cpz24
entries.doc: Current best upper bound [CPZ24]: $\alpha(n) \ll n^{-7/6 + o(1)}$.
entries.statement: ∃ (o : ℕ → ℝ), Tendsto o atTop (𝓝 0) ∧ α ≪ (fun n ↦ upperBarrier n * (n : ℝ) ^ o n)

entries.name: erdos_509.variants.Cartan_bound
entries.doc: Let $f(z) ∈ ℂ[z]$ be a monic non-constant polynomial. Can the set $\{z ∈ ℂ : |f(z)| ≤ 1\}$ be covered by a set of closed discs the sum of whose radii is $≤ 2e$? Solution: True. This is due to Cartan. See *Sur les systèmes de fonctions holomorphes à variétés linéaires lacunaires et leurs applications*, Henri Cartan, http://www.numdam.org/article/ASENS_1928_3_45__255_0.pdf
entries.statement: answer(True) ↔ ∀ (f : ℂ[X]), f.Monic → f.natDegree ≠ 0 → ∃ (ι : Type), Nonempty (BoundedDiscCover {z | ‖f.eval z‖ ≤ 1} (2*rexp 1) ι)

entries.name: erdos_509.variants.Pommerenke_bound
entries.doc: Let $f(z) ∈ $ℂ[z]$ be a monic non-constant polynomial. Can the set $\{z ∈ ℂ : |f(z)| ≤ 1\}$ be covered by a set of closed discs the sum of whose radii is $≤ 2.59$? Solution: True. This is due to Pommerenke.
entries.statement: answer(True) ↔ ∀ (f : ℂ[X]), f.Monic → f.natDegree ≠ 0 → ∃ (ι : Type), Nonempty (BoundedDiscCover {z | ‖f.eval z‖ ≤ 1} 2.59 ι)

entries.name: erdos_509.variants.Pommerenke_connected
entries.doc: Let $f(z) ∈ ℂ[z]$ be a monic non-constant polynomial. If it is connected, can the set $\{z ∈ ℂ : |f(z)| ≤ 1\}$ be covered by a set of circles the sum of whose radii is $≤ 2$? Solution: True. This is due to Pommerenke.
entries.statement: answer(True) ↔ ∀ (f : ℂ[X]), f.Monic → f.natDegree ≠ 0 → IsConnected {z | ‖f.eval z‖ ≤ 1} → ∃ (ι : Type), Nonempty (BoundedDiscCover {z | ‖f.eval z‖ ≤ 1} 2 ι)

entries.name: erdos_513.variants.upper_bound
entries.doc: For all transcendental entire function `f`, `liminf (fun r : ℝ => ratio r f) atTop ≤ 2 / π - c` for some `c > 0`. This is proved in [ClHa64].
entries.statement: ∃ c > 0, ⨆ f : {f : ℂ → ℂ // Transcendental ℂ[X] f ∧ Differentiable ℂ f}, (liminf (fun r : ℝ => ratio r f) atTop) ≤ 2 / π - c

entries.name: erdos_513.variants.lower_bound
entries.doc: For all transcendental entire function `f`, `liminf (fun r : ℝ => ratio r f) atTop > 1 / 2`.
entries.statement: ⨆ f : {f : ℂ → ℂ // Transcendental ℂ[X] f ∧ Differentiable ℂ f}, (liminf (fun r : ℝ => ratio r f) atTop) > 1 / 2

entries.name: erdos_516
entries.doc: An entire function `f` is said to be of finite order if there exist numbers c, a ≥ 0 such that for all `z`, `‖f z‖ ≤ c * rexp (‖z‖ ^ a)`. -/ def OfFiniteOrder {E F: Type*} [NormedAddCommGroup E] [NormedSpace ℂ E] [NormedAddCommGroup F] [NormedSpace ℂ F] (f : E → F) : Prop := Differentiable ℂ f ∧ ∃ c ≥ 0, ∃ a ≥ 0, ∀ z, ‖f z‖ ≤ c * rexp (‖z‖ ^ a) noncomputable def ratio (r : ℝ) (f : ℂ → ℂ) : ℝ := (⨅
entries.statement: ℂ → ℂ} {n : ℕ → ℕ} (hn : HasFabryGaps n) {a : ℕ → ℂ} (ha : ∀ n, a n ≠ 0) (hfn : ∀ z, HasSum (fun k => a k * z ^ n k) (f z)) (hf : OfFiniteOrder f) : limsup (fun r => ratio r f) atTop = 1

entries.name: erdos_516.variants.limsup_ratio_eq_one
entries.doc: Let `f = ∑ aₖzⁿₖ` be an entire function such that `nₖ > k (log k) ^ (2 + c)`. Then `limsup (fun r => ratio r f) atTop = 1`. This is proved in [Ko65].
entries.statement: ℂ → ℂ} {n : ℕ → ℕ} (hn : ∃ c > (0 : ℝ), ∀ k, n k > k * log k ^ (2 + c)) {a : ℕ → ℂ} (ha : ∀ n, a n ≠ 0) (hfn : ∀ z, HasSum (fun k => a k * z ^ n k) (f z)) : limsup (fun r => ratio r f) atTop = 1

entries.name: erdos_596.variants.C4_C6_is_exceptional
entries.doc: The pair $(C_4, C_6)$ is Erdős–Hajnal exceptional; combines `C4_C6_finite_ramsey` and `C4_free_countable_escape`.
entries.statement: IsErdosHajnalExceptional (cycleGraph 4) (cycleGraph 6) := ⟨erdos_596.variants.C4_C6_finite_ramsey, erdos_596.variants.C4_free_countable_escape⟩ /-- The original Erdős–Hajnal conjecture (that no exceptional pair exists) is **false** — witnessed by $(C_4, C_6)$ via `C4_C6_is_exceptional`. -/ @[category research solved, AMS 5] theorem erdos_596.variants.original_conjecture_is_false : answer(False) ↔ ∀ {U₁ U₂ : Type} (G₁ : SimpleGraph U₁) (G₂ : SimpleGraph U₂), ¬IsErdosHajnalExceptional G₁ G₂

entries.name: disjoint_without_infinite_claim.disproof
entries.doc: The empty family vacuously has Property B, exercising `erdos_602.variants.empty_index`. -/ @[category test, AMS 3 5] example : HasPropertyB PEmpty (fun (_ : PEmpty) => (Set.univ : Set ℕ)) := erdos_602.variants.empty_index _ (fun _ => Set.infinite_univ) /-- Any infinite set, viewed as a singleton family, has Property B, exercising `erdos_602.variants.unique_index`. -/ @[category test, AMS 3 5] exam
entries.statement: ¬ disjoint_without_infinite_claim

entries.name: erdos_rado
entries.doc: **Erdős–Rado partial result**: $\mathfrak{c} \to (\omega + n, 4)^3_2$ for any $2 \le n < \omega$. Positive partial answer to Problem 70 with $\beta = \omega + n$ and the blue side fixed at $4$.
entries.statement: ℕ) (hn : 2 ≤ n) : OrdinalCardinalRamsey3 (𝔠).ord (ω + n) 4

entries.name: erdos_755
entries.doc: A three-point set whose pairwise distances are all equal to `side`. -/ def IsEquilateralTriangle {d : ℕ} (side : ℝ) (T : Finset (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin d))) : Prop := T.card = 3 ∧ ∀ p ∈ T, ∀ q ∈ T, p ≠ q → dist p q = side /-- A unit equilateral triangle in Euclidean `d`-space. -/ def IsUnitEquilateralTriangle {d : ℕ} (T : Finset (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin d))) : Prop := IsEquilateralTriangle 1 T /-- An
entries.statement: answer(True) ↔ ∃ o : ℕ → ℝ, o =o[atTop] (fun _ : ℕ => (1 : ℝ)) ∧ ∀ᶠ n in atTop, (TUnit 6 n : ℝ) ≤ ((1 / 27 : ℝ) + o n) * (n : ℝ) ^ 3

entries.name: erdos_755.variants.any_size_cdl
entries.doc: Clemen, Dumitrescu, and Liu [CDL25b] proved the stronger version where equilateral triangles of all positive side lengths are counted.
entries.statement: (fun n : ℕ => (TAnySize 6 n : ℝ)) ~[atTop] (fun n : ℕ => (1 / 27 : ℝ) * (n : ℝ) ^ 3)

entries.name: erdos_786.parts.i.selfridge
entries.doc: An example of such a set with density $\frac 1 4$ is given by the integers $\equiv 2\pmod{4}$ -/ @[category textbook, AMS 11] theorem erdos_786.parts.i.example (A : Set ℕ) (hA : A = { n | n % 4 = 2 }) : A.HasDensity (1 / 4) ∧ A.IsMulCardSet := by sorry /-- `consecutivePrimesFrom p k` gives the set of `k + 1` consecutive primes that are at least `p` in size. If `p` is prime then this is the set of
entries.statement: ℝ) (hε : 0 < ε ∧ ε < 1 / rexp 1) : ∀ᶠ (p : ℕ) in atTop, p.Prime → ∃ k, ∑ q ∈ consecutivePrimesFrom p k, (1 : ℝ) / q < 1 ∧ 1 < ∑ q ∈ consecutivePrimesFrom p (k + 1), (1 : ℝ) / q ∧ letI A := { n | ∑ q ∈ consecutivePrimesFrom p k, (n : ℕ).factorization q = 1 } A.HasDensity (1 / rexp 1 - ε) ∧ A.IsMulCardSet

entries.name: johnson_chromaticNumber_odd
entries.doc: It can be seen that the chromatic number of $J(2k,k)$ is $>k+1$ for all odd $k>2$.
entries.statement: ℕ) (hk : 2 < k) (h : Odd k) : k + 1 < J(2 * k, k).chromaticNumber := sorry /-- Ma and Tang have proved that the chromatic number of $J(2k,k)$ is $>k+1$ for all $k>2$ not of the form $p-1$ for prime $p$. -/ @[category research solved, AMS 5] theorem johnson_chromaticNumber_composite (k : ℕ) (hk : 2 < k) (h : (k + 1).Composite) : k + 1 < J(2 * k, k).chromaticNumber := sorry /-- Ma and Tang's result implies the cases for odd $k$. -/ @[category test, AMS 5] theorem johnsonGraph_chromaticNumber_odd_of_johnson_chromaticNumber_composite : (type_of% johnson_chromaticNumber_composite) → (type_of% johnson_chromaticNumber_odd)

entries.name: erdos_868.parts.i
entries.doc: The number of ways in which a natural `n` can be written as the sum of `o` members of the set `A`. -/ noncomputable def ncard_add_repr (A : Set ℕ) (o : ℕ) (n : ℕ) : ℕ := { a : Fin o → ℕ | Set.range a ⊆ A ∧ ∑ i, a i = n }.ncard /-- Let $A$ be an additive basis of order $2$, let $f(n)$ denote the number of ways in which $n$ can be written as the sum of two elements from $A$. If $f(n) \to \infty$ as
entries.statement: answer(False) ↔ ∀ (A : Set ℕ), A.IsAsymptoticAddBasisOfOrder 2 → atTop.Tendsto (fun n => ncard_add_repr A 2 n) atTop → ∃ B ⊆ A, B.IsAsymptoticAddBasisOfOrder 2 ∧ ∀ b ∈ B, ¬(B \ {b}).IsAsymptoticAddBasisOfOrder 2

entries.name: erdos_868.parts.ii
entries.doc: Let $A$ be an additive basis of order $2$, let $f(n)$ denote the number of ways in which $n$ can be written as the sum of two elements from $A$. If $f(n) > \epsilon \log n$ for large $n$ and an arbitrary fixed $\epsilon > 0$, then must $A$ contain a minimal additive basis of order $2$? Larsen and Larsen [LaLa26] constructed a counterexample with $f(n) > c \log n$ for all large $n$.
entries.statement: answer(False) ↔ ∀ᵉ (A : Set ℕ) (ε > 0), A.IsAsymptoticAddBasisOfOrder 2 → (∀ᶠ (n : ℕ) in atTop, ε * Real.log n < ncard_add_repr A 2 n) → ∃ B ⊆ A, B.IsAsymptoticAddBasisOfOrder 2 ∧ ∀ b ∈ B, ¬(B \ {b}).IsAsymptoticAddBasisOfOrder 2

entries.name: erdos_868.variants.fixed_
entries.doc: Erdős and Nathanson proved that this is true if $f(n) > (\log \frac{4}{3})^{-1} \log n$ for all large $n$.
entries.statement: answer(True) ↔ ∀ (A : Set ℕ), A.IsAsymptoticAddBasisOfOrder 2 → (∀ᶠ (n : ℕ) in atTop, (Real.log (4 / 3))⁻¹ * Real.log n < ncard_add_repr A 2 n) → ∃ B ⊆ A, B.IsAsymptoticAddBasisOfOrder 2 ∧ ∀ b ∈ B, ¬(B \ {b}).IsAsymptoticAddBasisOfOrder 2

entries.name: erdos_90
entries.doc: The set of all possible numbers of unit distances for a configuration of $n$ points. -/ noncomputable def unitDistanceCounts (n : ℕ) : Set ℕ := {unitDistancePairsCount points | (points : Finset ℝ²) (_ : points.card = n)} /-- This lemma confirms that the set of possible unit distance counts is bounded above, which ensures that taking the supremum (`sSup`) is a well-defined operation. The trivial up
entries.statement: answer(False) ↔ ∃ (O : ℕ → ℝ) (hO : O =O[atTop] (fun n => 1 / (n : ℝ).log.log)), (fun n => (maxUnitDistances n : ℝ)) =ᶠ[atTop] fun (n : ℕ) => (n : ℝ) ^ (1 + O n)

entries.name: erdos_90.variants.polynomial_lower_bound
entries.doc: **Constructive form of the disproof.** There is an absolute constant $c > 0$ such that infinitely many $n$ admit a configuration realising at least $n^{1+c}$ unit distances. This is the qualitative content of Theorem 1.1 of Alon–Bloom–Gowers–Litt–Sawin–Shankar– Tsimerman–Wang–Matchett Wood, [*Remarks on the disproof of the unit distance conjecture*](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20695) (2026). An exp
entries.statement: ∃ c > (0 : ℝ), {n : ℕ | (n : ℝ) ^ (1 + c) ≤ (maxUnitDistances n : ℝ)}.Infinite

entries.name: erdos_90.variants.sawin_explicit
entries.doc: **Sawin's explicit exponent.** The constructive disproof can be realised with $c \ge 0.014114$ (absorbing the implicit constant $C$ of Sawin's Theorem 1 into a slightly smaller exponent for all large enough $n$). Reference: Theorem 1 of Sawin, [arXiv:2605.20579](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20579) (2026).
entries.statement: {n : ℕ | (n : ℝ) ^ (1.014114 : ℝ) ≤ (maxUnitDistances n : ℝ)}.Infinite

entries.name: erdos_91.variants.three
entries.doc: For $n = 3$ the equilateral triangle is the only such set.
entries.statement: UniqueMinimizer 3

entries.name: erdos_91.variants.four
entries.doc: For $n=4$ the square or two equilateral triangles sharing an edge give two non-similar examples.
entries.statement: ¬ UniqueMinimizer 4

entries.name: erdos_91.variants.five
entries.doc: For $n = 5$ the regular pentagon is the unique such set (which has two distinct distances). Erdős mysteriously remarks in [Er90] this was proved by 'a colleague'. (In [Er87b] this is described as 'a colleague from Zagreb (unfortunately I do not have his letter)'.) A published proof of this fact is provided by Kovács [Ko24c].
entries.statement: UniqueMinimizer 5

entries.name: erdos_91.variants.six
entries.doc: In [Er87b] on p.171 Erdős says that there are at least two non-similar examples for $n = 6$.
entries.statement: ¬ UniqueMinimizer 6

entries.name: erdos_97.variants.three_equidistant
entries.doc: Erdős originally conjectured this (in [Er46b]) with no 3 vertices equidistant, but Danzer found a convex polygon on 9 points such that every vertex has three vertices equidistant from it (but this distance depends on the vertex). Danzer's construction is explained in [Er87b]. [Er46b] Erdős, P., _On sets of distances of $n$ points_. Amer. Math. Monthly (1946), 248-250. [Er87b] Erdős, P., _Some comb
entries.statement: ∃ A : Finset ℝ², A.Nonempty ∧ ConvexIndep A ∧ HasNEquidistantProperty 3 A

entries.name: erdos_97.variants.three_unit_distance
entries.doc: Fishburn and Reeds [FiRe92] have found a convex polygon on 20 points such that every vertex has three vertices equidistant from it (and this distance is the same for all vertices). [FiRe92] Fishburn, P. C. and Reeds, J. A., _Unit distances between vertices of a convex polygon_. Comput. Geom. (1992), 81-91.
entries.statement: ∃ A : Finset ℝ², A.Nonempty ∧ ConvexIndep A ∧ HasNUnitDistanceProperty 3 A

entries.name: erdos_97.variants.three_unit_distance_cut_min

entries.name: erdos_98.variants.upper_bound
entries.doc: Erdős could not even prove $h(n)\geq n$. Pach has shown $h(n) < n^{\log_2 3}$. Erdős, Füredi, and Pach [EFPR93] have improved this to $h(n) < n\exp(c\sqrt{\log n})$ for some constant $c>0$.
entries.statement: ∃ c > (0 : ℝ), ∀ᶠ n : ℕ in atTop, (h n : ℝ) < (n : ℝ) * Real.exp (c * Real.sqrt (Real.log (n : ℝ)))

entries.name: green_18.bmz_corners
entries.doc: The number of triples $(x, y, g)$ in $G^3$ such that $g \neq e$, and $(x, y), (xg, y), (x, gy)$ are all in $A$. These are called "BMZ corners" by [Au16]. -/ def numBmzCorners {G : Type*} [Group G] [Fintype G] [DecidableEq G] (A : Finset (G × G)) : ℕ := ( (univ : Finset (G × G × G)).filter fun ⟨x, y, g⟩ => g ≠ 1 ∧ (x, y) ∈ A ∧ (x * g, y) ∈ A ∧ (x, g * y) ∈ A ).card /-- [So13] proved this is true fo
entries.statement: ∀ α > 0, ∃ c > 0, ∃ m₀ : ℕ, ∀ (G : Type*) [Group G] [Fintype G] [DecidableEq G] (A : Finset (G × G)), Fintype.card G ≥ m₀ → (A.card : ℝ) ≥ α * (Fintype.card G) ^ 2 → (numBmzCorners A : ℝ) ≥ c * (Fintype.card G) ^ 3

entries.name: hilbert_17th_problem_poly
entries.doc: The statement is false in general if we restrict to polynomials. The polynomial (by Motzkin) $f(x, y) = x^4 y^2 + x^2 y^4 - 3 x^2 y^2 + 1$ takes only nonnegative values but cannot be written as a sum of squares of polynomials. -/ noncomputable def f : MvPolynomial (Fin 2) ℝ := X 0 ^ 4 * X 1 ^ 2 + X 0 ^ 2 * X 1 ^ 4 - 3 * X 0 ^ 2 * X 1 ^ 2 + 1 -- Proof taken from `motzkin_polynomial_nonneg` in mathl

entries.name: blochConstant_lower_bound
entries.doc: The **Bloch radius** $B_f$ of a function $f$ is the supremum of radii of univalent disks in the image of the unit disk under $f$. Takes values in `ℝ≥0∞` so that functions whose image contains arbitrarily large univalent disks correctly get radius `⊤` rather than `0`. -/ noncomputable def blochRadius (f : ℂ → ℂ) : ℝ≥0∞ := sSup (ENNReal.ofReal '' {r : ℝ | ∃ S ⊆ ball (0 : ℂ) 1, ∃ x, ball x r ⊆ f '' S
entries.statement: Real.sqrt 3 / 4 + 2 * 10 ^ (-4 : ℤ) ≤ blochConstant

entries.name: blochConstant_upper_bound
entries.doc: It is proved in [AG37] that the Bloch constant is bounded above by $\frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + \sqrt{3}}}\frac{\Gamma(1/3) \Gamma(11/12)}{\Gamma(1/4)}$$.
entries.statement: blochConstant ≤ Real.Gamma (1 / 3) * Real.Gamma (11 / 12) / (Real.Gamma (1 / 4) * Real.sqrt (1 + Real.sqrt 3))

entries.name: univalentBlochConstant_lower_bound
entries.doc: The **Univalent Bloch constant** $B_u$ is the largest radius such that every univalent holomorphic function on the unit disk with $f'(0) = 1$ has a schlicht disk of that radius in its image. -/ noncomputable def univalentBlochConstant : ℝ := sSup {B : ℝ | ∀ f : ℂ → ℂ, InjOn f (ball 0 1) → DifferentiableOn ℂ f (ball 0 1) → deriv f 0 = 1 → ∃ S ⊆ ball 0 1, ∃ x, ball x B ⊆ f '' S ∧ InjOn f S} /-- It i
entries.statement: 0.5708858 ≤ univalentBlochConstant

entries.name: univalentBlochConstant_upper_bound
entries.doc: The Univalent Bloch constant is trivially bounded above by the Bloch radius of the identity function, which is $1$. This is the best upper bound we know according to [OptimizationConstants].
entries.statement: univalentBlochConstant ≤ 1

entries.name: landauConstant_lower_bound
entries.doc: The **Landau constant** $L$ is the largest radius such that every holomorphic function on the unit disk with $f'(0) = 1$ has a disk of that radius contained in its image. -/ noncomputable def landauConstant : ℝ := sSup {B : ℝ | ∀ f : ℂ → ℂ, DifferentiableOn ℂ f (ball 0 1) → deriv f 0 = 1 → ∃ x, ball x B ⊆ f '' (ball 0 1)} /-- It is proved in [Ya95] that the Landau constant is bounded below by $0.5
entries.statement: 0.5 + 10 ^ (-335 : ℤ) ≤ landauConstant

entries.name: landauConstant_upper_bound
entries.doc: It is proved in [Ra43] that the Landau constant is bounded above by $\frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + \sqrt{3}}}\frac{\Gamma(1/3) \Gamma(5/6)}{\Gamma(1/6)}$.
entries.statement: landauConstant ≤ Real.Gamma (1 / 3) * Real.Gamma (5 / 6) / Real.Gamma (1 / 6)

entries.name: Invariant_subspace_problem_finite_dimensional
entries.doc: Every (bounded) linear operator `T : H → H` on a finite-dimensional linear space `H` of dimension at least 2 has a non-trivial (closed) `T`-invariant subspace. This can be solved using the Jordan normal form, which is [not yet in mathlib](https://leanprover-community.github.io/undergrad_todo.html).

entries.name: Invariant_subspace_problem_normal_operator
entries.doc: Every bounded linear operator `T : H → H` on a non-separable Hilbert space `H` has a non-trivial closed `T`-invariant subspace. Such an invariant space is given by considering the closure of the linear span of the orbit of any single non-zero vector. -/ @[category research solved, AMS 47, formal_proof using formal_conjectures at ""] theorem Invariant_subspace_problem_non_separable [InnerProductSpa
entries.statement: 2 ≤ Module.rank ℂ H) (T : H →L[ℂ] H) [IsStarNormal T]: Nonempty (ClosedInvariantSubspace T)

entries.name: kakeya_3d
entries.doc: The three-dimensional case, proved by Wang, Zahl [WaZa25]. [WaZa25] Wang, H. and Zahl, J., _Volume estimates for unions of convex sets, and the Kakeya set conjecture in three dimensions_. arXiv preprint, arXiv:2502.17655, 2025.
entries.statement: KakeyaSetConjectureDim 3

entries.name: kakeya_finite
entries.doc: A finite field variant of the Kakeya problem considers subsets of `𝔽_qⁿ` that contain a line in every direction. -/ def IsKakeyaFinite {F : Type*} [Field F] [Fintype F] {n : ℕ} (S : Finset (Fin n → F)) : Prop := ∀ v, v ≠ 0 → ∃ a, ∀ t : F, a + t • v ∈ S open Fintype in /-- The finite field Kakeya conjecture asserts that any Kakeya set in `𝔽_qⁿ` has size at least `c_n · qⁿ` for some constant `c_n` d
entries.statement: Type*} [Field F] [Fintype F] {n : ℕ} (K : Finset (Fin n → F)) (hK : IsKakeyaFinite K) : card F ^ n / (2 - 1 / card F : ℚ) ^ (n - 1) ≤ K.card

entries.name: UnitConjecture.counterexamples.i
entries.doc: A unit in `K[G]` is trivial if it is exactly of the form `kg` where: - `k` is a unit in the base field `K` - `g` is an element of the group `G` -/ def IsTrivialUnit (u : MonoidAlgebra K G) : Prop := ∃ (k : Kˣ) (g : G), u = MonoidAlgebra.single g (k : K) omit hG @[category API, AMS 16 20] lemma IsTrivialUnit.isUnit {u : MonoidAlgebra K G} (h : IsTrivialUnit u) : IsUnit u := by obtain ⟨k, g, rfl⟩ :=
entries.statement: ℕ) [hp : Fact p.Prime] : ∃ (u : (MonoidAlgebra (ZMod p) PromislowGroup)ˣ), ¬IsTrivialUnit u.val

entries.name: UnitConjecture.counterexamples.ii
entries.doc: If $P$ is the Promislow group, then the group ring $\mathbb{C}[P]$ has a non-trivial unit.
entries.statement: ∃ (u : (MonoidAlgebra ℂ PromislowGroup)ˣ), ¬IsTrivialUnit u.val

entries.name: amitsur_conjecture
entries.doc: The **Amitsur Conjecture**: If `J` is a nil ideal in `R`, then `J[x]` is a nil ideal of the polynomial ring `R[x]`. This is known to be false, see Agata Smoktunowicz, _Polynomial rings over nil rings need not be nil_.
entries.statement: TwoSidedIdeal R) (hJ : IsNil J) : IsNil (TwoSidedIdeal.map (Polynomial.C) J)

entries.name: abelian_is_leinster_iff_cyclic_perfect
entries.doc: Cyclic groups of perfect number order are Leinster groups. This follows from the fact that for a cyclic group, all subgroups are normal and correspond to divisors of the group order, and a number is perfect if and only if the sum of its divisors (including itself) equals twice the number. -/ @[category API, AMS 20] theorem cyclic_of_perfect_is_leinster (G : Type*) [Group G] [Fintype G] [IsCyclic G
entries.statement: Type*) [CommGroup G] [Fintype G] : IsLeinster G ↔ IsCyclic G ∧ Nat.Perfect (Fintype.card G)

entries.name: exists_nonabelian_leinster_group
entries.doc: Non-abelian Leinster groups exist. For example, `S₃ × C₅` (order 30) and `A₅ × C₁₅₁₂₈` are Leinster groups. Reference: Leinster, Tom (2001). "Perfect numbers and groups".
entries.statement: ∃ G : Type, ∃ (_ : Group G) (_ : Fintype G), IsLeinster G ∧ ¬ ∀ (a b : G), a * b = b * a

entries.name: dihedral_is_leinster_iff_odd_perfect
entries.doc: The dihedral group `DihedralGroup n` (of order `2n`) is a Leinster group if and only if `n` is an odd perfect number. This gives a one-to-one correspondence between dihedral Leinster groups and odd perfect numbers. In particular, the existence of odd perfect numbers is equivalent to the existence of dihedral Leinster groups. Reference: Leinster, Tom (2001). "Perfect numbers and groups".
entries.statement: ℕ) [NeZero n] : IsLeinster (DihedralGroup n) ↔ Nat.Perfect n ∧ Odd n

entries.name: mosers_worm_problem_upper_bound
entries.doc: There is a set of area 0.260437 that covers all worms. *Reference:* Norwood, Rick; Poole, George (2003), "An improved upper bound for Leo Moser's worm problem", Discrete and Computational Geometry, 29 (3): 409–417, doi:10.1007/s00454-002-0774-3, MR 1961007.
entries.statement: ∃ X ∈ WormCovers, volume X = 0.260437

entries.name: convex_mosers_worm_problem_bound_attained
entries.doc: The minimal area of a convex shape that can cover every unit-length curve is attained. This follows from the Blaschke selection theorem.
entries.statement: ∃ bound, IsLeast {v | ∃ X ∈ WormCovers, Convex ℝ X ∧ volume X = v} bound

entries.name: convex_mosers_worm_problem_upper_bound
entries.doc: There is a convex set of area 0.270911861 that covers all worms. *Reference:* Wang, Wei (2006), "An improved upper bound for the worm problem", Acta Mathematica Sinica, 49 (4): 835–846, MR 2264090.
entries.statement: ∃ X : Set ℝ², MeasurableSet X ∧ Convex ℝ X ∧ volume X = 0.270911861 ∧ ∀ w ∈ Worms, ∃ (e : ℝ² ≃ₗᵢ[ℝ] ℝ²) (v : ℝ²), e.toLinearEquiv.det = 1 ∧ w ⊆ (fun x => e x + v) '' X

entries.name: convex_mosers_worm_problem_lower_bound
entries.doc: 0.232239 is a lower bound on the area of a convex set that covers all worms. *Reference:* Khandhawit, Tirasan; Pagonakis, Dimitrios; Sriswasdi, Sira (2013), "Lower Bound for Convex Hull Area and Universal Cover Problems", International Journal of Computational Geometry & Applications, 23 (3): 197–212, arXiv:1101.5638, doi:10.1142/S0218195913500076, MR 3158583, S2CID 207132316.
entries.statement: 0.232239 ∈ lowerBounds {v | ∃ X ∈ WormCovers, Convex ℝ X ∧ volume X = v}

entries.name: sofaConstant_eq_volume_gerversSofa
entries.doc: Gerver's sofa attains the sofa constant, conjectured by [Ge92] and claimed by [Ba24].
entries.statement: sofaConstant = volume gerversSofa

entries.name: noether_problem
entries.doc: A rational field extension is a field extension `L/K` isomorphic to a field of rational functions (in some arbitrary number of indeterminates.) -/ class IsRationalExtension (K L ι : Type*) [Field K] [Field L] [Algebra K L] where pure_transcendental : Nonempty (L ≃ₐ[K] ((FractionRing (MvPolynomial ι K)))) /-- If the index set `ι` is empty, then `IsRationalExtension K L ι` means that `K, L` are isom
entries.statement: answer(False) ↔ ∀ (K L ι G : Type) [Field K] [Field L] [Fintype ι] [Algebra K L] [IsRationalExtension K L ι], HasNoetherProperty K L ι

entries.name: noether_problem.variants.two
entries.doc: The Noether problem has a positive solution in the two indeterminate case.
entries.statement: Type} [Field K] [Field L] [Fintype ι] [Algebra K L] [IsRationalExtension K L ι] (hι : Fintype.card ι = 2) : HasNoetherProperty K L ι

entries.name: noether_problem.variants.three
entries.doc: The Noether problem has a positive solution in the three indeterminate case.
entries.statement: Type} [Field K] [Field L] [Fintype ι] [Algebra K L] [IsRationalExtension K L ι] (hι : Fintype.card ι = 3) : HasNoetherProperty K L ι

entries.name: noether_problem.variants.four
entries.doc: The Noether problem has a positive solution in the four indeterminate case.
entries.statement: Type} [Field K] [Field L] [Fintype ι] [Algebra K L] [IsRationalExtension K L ι] (hι : Fintype.card ι = 4) : HasNoetherProperty K L ι

entries.name: noether_problem.variants.forty_seven
entries.doc: One can find a counterexample to the Noether Problem's claim by considering a rational function field in 47 indeterminates.
entries.statement: ∃ (K L ι G : Type) (_ : Field K) (_ : Field L) (_ : Fintype ι) (_ : Algebra K L) (_ : IsRationalExtension K L ι), Fintype.card ι = 47 ∧ ¬ HasNoetherProperty K L ι

entries.name: pierce_birkhoff_conjecture_dim_one
entries.doc: The Pierce-Birkhoff conjecture holds for `n = 1`. This was proved by Louis Mahé.
entries.statement: ℝ → ℝ) (hf : IsPiecewisePolynomial f) : ∃ (ι κ : Type) (g : ι → κ → Polynomial ℝ), Finite ι ∧ Finite κ ∧ ∀ x, f x = ⨆ i, ⨅ j, Polynomial.eval x (g i j)

entries.name: pierce_birkhoff_conjecture_dim_two
entries.doc: The Pierce-Birkhoff conjecture holds for `n = 2`. This was proved by Louis Mahé.
entries.statement: (Fin 2 → ℝ) → ℝ) (hf : IsPiecewiseMvPolynomial f) : ∃ (ι κ : Type) (g : ι → κ → MvPolynomial (Fin 2) ℝ), Finite ι ∧ Finite κ ∧ ∀ x, f x = ⨆ i, ⨅ j, MvPolynomial.eval x (g i j)

entries.name: conjecture36
entries.doc: `dp G` is the number of diametrical pairs of `G`: the number of unordered pairs `{u, v}` of vertices at distance `diam(G)`. -/ noncomputable def dp {α : Type*} [Fintype α] (G : SimpleGraph α) : ℕ := (Finset.univ.filter (fun p : Sym2 α => p.lift ⟨fun u v => G.dist u v = G.diam ∧ u ≠ v, fun u v => by simp [SimpleGraph.dist_comm, ne_comm]⟩)).card /-- WOWII [Conjecture 36](http://cms.uhd.edu/faculty/d
entries.statement: answer(False) ↔ ∀ {α : Type*} [Fintype α] [DecidableEq α] [Nontrivial α], ∀ (G : SimpleGraph α) [DecidableRel G.Adj] (_ : G.Connected) (_ : 0 < dp G), (2 * G.radius.toNat : ℝ) / (dp G : ℝ) ≤ (path G : ℝ)

entries.name: erdos_416.variants.Maier_Pomerance
entries.doc: Let `V(x)` count the number of `n≤x` such that `ϕ(m)=n` is solvable. `V(x)=x/logx * e^((C+o(1))(log log log x)^2)`, for some explicit constant `C>0`. Ref:Maier, Helmut and Pomerance, Carl, _On the number of distinct values of Euler's $\phi$-function_.
entries.statement: let C : ℝ := answer(sorry) 0 < C ∧ ∃ f : ℝ → ℝ, f =o[atTop] (1 : ℝ → ℝ) ∧ ∀ᶠ x in Filter.atTop, (V x : ℝ) = x / x.log * (rexp <| (C + f x) * x.log.log.log ^ 2)

entries.name: erdos_975.variants.quadratic
entries.doc: When $f$ is an irreducible quadratic polynomial, the question is answered first by Hooley [Ho63]. More compact expression of the constant in terms of Hurwitz class numbers (when $a = 1$) is given by McKey in [Mc95], [Mc97], [Mc99]. TODO: formalize Hurwitz class numbers and the expression of the constant in terms of them.
entries.statement: ℤ[X]) (hf : Irreducible f) (hf_pos : ∀ᶠ n : ℕ in atTop, 1 ≤ f.eval ↑n) (hf_degree : f.degree = 2) (c : ℝ) : c = answer(sorry) → 0 < c ∧ Tendsto (fun x ↦ Erdos975Sum f x / (x * log x)) atTop (𝓝 c)

entries.name: green_14_lower_bound_green
entries.doc: [Gr21] proved a lower bound of shape $W(3, r) \gg \exp(c(\log r)^{4/3-o(1)})$.
entries.statement: answer(sorry) ↔ ∃ c : ℝ, ∃ (o : ℕ → ℝ) (_ : Tendsto o atTop (𝓝 0)), (fun (r : ℕ) => Real.exp (c * (Real.log r)^(4/3 - o r))) =O[atTop] fun r => (W 3 r : ℝ)

entries.name: green_14_lower_bound_hunter
entries.doc: [Hu22] improved this to $W(3, r) \gg \exp(c(\log r)^{2-o(1)})$.
entries.statement: answer(sorry) ↔ ∃ c : ℝ, ∃ (o : ℕ → ℝ) (_ : Tendsto o atTop (𝓝 0)), (fun (r : ℕ) => Real.exp (c * (Real.log r)^(2 - o r))) =O[atTop] (fun r => (W 3 r : ℝ))

entries.name: green_14_lower_bound_brown_landman_robertson
entries.doc: [BLR08] proved $W(3, r) \gg r^{2 - 1/\log \log r}$.
entries.statement: answer(sorry) ↔ (fun (r : ℕ) => (r : ℝ)^(2 - 1 / Real.log (Real.log r))) =O[atTop] (fun r => (W 3 r : ℝ))

entries.name: green_14_lower_bound_li_shu
entries.doc: [LiSh10] proved $W(3, r) \gg (r / \log r)^2$.
entries.statement: answer(sorry) ↔ (fun (r : ℕ) => ((r : ℝ) / Real.log r)^2) =O[atTop] (fun r => (W 3 r : ℝ))

entries.name: green_14_upper_bound_schoen
entries.doc: [Sc20] proves the upper bound $W(3, r) < \exp(r^{1-c})$ for some $c > 0$.
entries.statement: answer(sorry) ↔ ∃ c : ℝ, 0 < c ∧ (fun (r : ℕ) => ((W 3 r) : ℝ)) =O[atTop] (fun r => Real.exp ((r : ℝ) ^ (1 - c)))

entries.name: green_14_upper_bound_kelley_meka
entries.doc: [KeMe23] gives a corresponding upper bound $W(3, r) \ll \exp(C(\log r)^C)$.
entries.statement: answer(sorry) ↔ ∃ C : ℝ, (fun (r : ℕ) => ((W 3 r) : ℝ)) =O[atTop] (fun r => Real.exp (C * (Real.log r)^C))

entries.name: conjecture_1_4
entries.doc: **Conjecture 1.4**: if $\mu$ is an atomless $T_p$-invariant Borel probability measure on $\mathbb{T}$, then $T_{q^n}\mu$ converges weak-star to Lebesgue measure. This paper disproves the conjecture.
entries.statement: answer(False) ↔ ∀ p q : ℕ, 2 <= p → 2 <= q → MultiplicativelyIndependent p q → ∀ μ : ProbabilityMeasure 𝕋, MeasureTheory.IsAtomLess μ.1 → MeasurePreserving (Tn p) μ μ → Tendsto (fun n : ℕ => μ.map (Tn_continuous (q ^ n)).aemeasurable) atTop (𝓝 UnitAddCircle.ProbabilityMeasure)

entries.name: erdos_1085.variants.upper_lower_d5_odd
entries.doc: Erdős and Pach showed that, for $d \ge 5$ odd, there exist constants $c_1(d), c_2(d) > 0$ such that $\frac{p - 1}{2p} n^2 - c_1 n^{4/3} ≤ f_d(n) \le \frac{p - 1}{2p} n^2 + c_2 n^{4/3}$ where $p = \lfloor\frac d2\rfloor$.

entries.name: erdos_522.variants.number_real_roots
entries.doc: Erdős and Offord showed that the number of real roots of a random degree `n` polynomial with `±1` coefficients is `(2/π+o(1))log n`.
entries.statement: ∃ p o : ℕ → ℝ, atTop.Tendsto o (𝓝 0) ∧ atTop.Tendsto p (𝓝 0) ∧ ∀ (Ω : Type*) [MeasureSpace Ω] [IsProbabilityMeasure (ℙ : Measure Ω)] (n : ℕ) (hn : 2 ≤ n) (f : KacCoefficients ({-1, 1} : Set ℝ) Ω), (ℙ {ω | |(f.roots n ω).card / (n : ℝ).log - 2 / π| ≥ o n}).toReal ≤ p n

entries.name: erdos_522.variants.yakir_solution
entries.doc: Yakir proved that almost all Kac polynomials have `n/2+O(n^(9/10))` many roots in `{z∈C:|z|≤1}`.
entries.statement: ∃ p : ℕ → ℝ, atTop.Tendsto p (𝓝 0) ∧ ∀ (Ω : Type*) [MeasureSpace Ω] [IsProbabilityMeasure (ℙ : Measure Ω)] (n : ℕ) (hn : 2 ≤ n) (f : KacCoefficients ({-1, 1} : Set ℂ) Ω), (ℙ {ω | |(f.roots n ω).countP (· ∈ Metric.closedBall 0 1) - (n / 2 : ℝ)| ≥ n^(9/10 : ℝ) }).toReal ≤ p n

entries.name: erdos_996.variants.log2
entries.doc: The following theorem is proved in [Ma66].
entries.statement: ∀ (C : ℝ), 0.5 < C → ∀ (f : Lp ℂ 2 (haarAddCircle (T := 1))) (n : ℕ → ℕ), IsLacunary n → (fun k => (eLpNorm (fourierPartial f k) 2 (haarAddCircle (T := 1))).toReal) =O[atTop] (fun k => 1 / (log (log k)) ^ C) → ∀ᵐ x, Tendsto (fun N => (∑ k ∈ .range N, f (n k • x)) / N) atTop (𝓝 (∫ t, f t ∂haarAddCircle))

entries.name: hilbert_smith_conjecture.variants.dimension_three
entries.doc: Pardon (2013): the Hilbert–Smith conjecture holds for 3-dimensional manifolds. See [arXiv:1112.2324](https://arxiv.org/abs/1112.2324).
entries.statement: Type*} [TopologicalSpace X] [T2Space X] [ConnectedSpace X] [ChartedSpace (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 3)) X] [IsTopologicalGroup G] [LocallyCompactSpace G] [MulAction G X] [ContinuousSMul G X] [FaithfulSMul G X] : AdmitsLieGroupStructure G

entries.name: Equation255_not_implies_Equation677
entries.doc: Equation 255 does not imply Equation 677.
entries.statement: ∃ (G : Type) (_ : Magma G), Equation255 G ∧ ¬ Equation677 G := ⟨Fin 3, ⟨![![1, 2, 0], ![2, 0, 1], ![0, 1, 2]]⟩, fun x ↦ by fin_cases x <;> rfl, of_decide_eq_false rfl⟩ /-- Equation 677 does not imply Equation 255. -/ @[category research solved, AMS 8] theorem Equation677_not_implies_Equation255 : ∃ (G : Type) (_ : Magma G), Equation677 G ∧ ¬ Equation255 G

entries.name: schur_truncatedExp_galoisGroup_equiv
entries.doc: The truncated exponential polynomial `truncatedExp n` is given by `∑_{j=0}^{n} x^j / j!` over `ℚ`, which is the `n`-th partial sum of the Taylor series for the exponential function `e^x`. -/ noncomputable def truncatedExp (n : ℕ) : ℚ[X] := ∑ j ∈ Finset.range (n + 1), (1 / j ! : ℚ) • X ^ j /-- **Schur's Theorem (1924):** Let `f_n(x) = ∑_{j=0}^n x^j/j!` be the `n`-th truncated exponential polynomial
entries.statement: ℕ) (hn : n ≥ 2) : letI f := truncatedExp n if n % 4 = 0 then -- Galois group is alternating group A_n Nonempty (f.Gal ≃* alternatingGroup (Fin n)) else -- Galois group is symmetric group S_n Nonempty (f.Gal ≃* Equiv.Perm (Fin n))

entries.name: voronovskaja_theorem.bernstein_operators
entries.doc: Cumulative sum $J_{n,k}(x) = \sum_{j=k}^n p_{n,j}(x)$. -/ noncomputable def bernsteinTail (n k : ℕ) : Polynomial ℝ := ∑ j ∈ Finset.Icc k n, bernsteinPolynomial ℝ n j /-- Bézier–type Bernstein operator: \[ (B_{n,\alpha} f)(x) = \sum_{k=0}^{n} f\!\left(\frac{k}{n}\right) \left( J_{n,k}(x)^{\alpha} - J_{n,k+1}(x)^{\alpha} \right) \] -/ noncomputable def bezierBernstein (n : ℕ) (α : ℝ) (f : ℝ → ℝ) (x
entries.statement: ℝ → ℝ) (x : ℝ) (hx : x ∈ I) (hf : ContDiffOn ℝ 2 f I) : let f'' : ℝ := iteratedDerivWithin 2 f I x Tendsto (fun (n : ℕ) => (n : ℝ) * (bezierBernstein n 1 f x - f x)) atTop (𝓝 ((1 / 2) * x * (1 - x) * f''))

entries.name: erdos_416.variants.Ford
entries.doc: Let `V(x)` count the number of `n≤x` such that `ϕ(m)=n` is solvable. `V(x) ≍ x/log x*e^(C_1*(log log log x − log log log log x)^2+C_2 log log log x − C_3 log log log log x)` Ref: Ford, Kevin, _The distribution of totients_.
entries.statement: let (C₁, C₂, C₃) : ℝ × ℝ × ℝ := answer(sorry) 0 < C₁ ∧ 0 < C₂ ∧ 0 < C₃ ∧ let G (x : ℝ) : ℝ := x / x.log * (rexp <| C₁ * (x.log.log.log - x.log.log.log.log) ^ 2 + C₂* x.log.log.log - C₃ * x.log.log.log.log) V =Θ[atTop] G

entries.name: sofaConstant_eq
entries.doc: The **horizontal side** of the hallway is $(-\infty, 1] \times [0, 1]$. -/ def horizontalHallway : Set ℝ² := {!₂[x, y] | (x) (y) (_ : x ≤ 1 ∧ 0 ≤ y ∧ y ≤ 1)} /-- The **vertical side** of the hallway is $[0, 1] \times (-\infty, 1]$. -/ def verticalHallway : Set ℝ² := {!₂[x, y] | (x) (y) (_ : 0 ≤ x ∧ x ≤ 1 ∧ y ≤ 1)} /-- The **hallway** is the union of its horizontal and vertical sides. -/ def hallwa
entries.statement: sofaConstant = answer(sorry)

entries.name: erdos_375.variants.legendre
entries.doc: In particular, if `Erdos375Prop` is true, then Legendre's conjecture is asymptotically true.
entries.statement: Erdos375Prop → (∀ᶠ n in atTop, ∃ p ∈ Set.Ioo (n ^ 2) ((n + 1) ^ 2), Nat.Prime p) := fun hp => LegendreConjecture.bounded_gap_legendre (erdos_375.variants.bounded_gap hp) /-- It is easy to see that for any `n ≥ 1` and `k ≤ 2`, if `n + 1, ..., n + k` are all composite, then there are distinct primes `p₁, ... pₖ` such that `pᵢ ∣ n + i` for all `1 ≤ i ≤ k`. -/ @[category research solved, AMS 11] theorem erdos_375.variants.le_two : ∀ n ≥ 1, ∀ k ≤ 2, (∀ i < k, ¬ (n + i + 1).Prime) → ∃ p : Fin k → ℕ, p.Injective ∧ ∀ i, (p i).Prime ∧ p i ∣ n + i + 1

entries.name: Finite.Equation255_not_implies_Equation677
entries.doc: Note that this is a stronger form of `Equation255_not_implies_Equation677`.
entries.statement: ∃ (G : Type) (_ : Magma G), Finite G ∧ Equation255 G ∧ ¬ Equation677 G := ⟨Fin 3, ⟨![![1, 2, 0], ![2, 0, 1], ![0, 1, 2]]⟩, Finite.intro (Fintype.equivFin _), fun x ↦ by fin_cases x <;> rfl, of_decide_eq_false rfl⟩ /-- The negation of `Finite.Equation677_implies_Equation255`. Probably this is true. It would be a stronger form of `Equation677_not_implies_Equation255`. Discussion thread here: https://leanprover.zulipchat.com/#narrow/channel/458659-Equational/topic/FINITE.3A.20677.20-.3E.20255 -/ @[category research open, AMS 8] theorem Finite.Equation677_not_implies_Equation255 : ∃ (G : Type) (_ : Magma G), Finite G ∧ Equation677 G ∧ ¬ Equation255 G
